proposals.title: Modulation of Rare Variant Penetrance in Ulcerative Colitis by Common Polygenic Risk: A Focus on the MUC2 Locus
proposals.motivation: The genetic architecture of ulcerative colitis (UC) involves both rare, high-impact variants and common, low-impact variants. While recent large-scale sequencing studies have identified rare variants in genes like MUC2, the substantial variability in disease presentation among carriers suggests that genetic background modifies their effect. Understanding how common polygenic risk scores (PRS) modulate the penetrance of rare variants is crucial for improving risk prediction and understanding disease mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the penetrance and phenotypic severity of rare, likely deleterious variants in the MUC2 gene are significantly modified by an individual's common variant-derived polygenic risk score for UC, with higher PRS amplifying the effect of the rare variant.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale biobank data (e.g., UK Biobank) with whole-exome/genome sequencing and imputed genotype data. We will identify carriers of rare (MAF<0.1%), predicted deleterious variants in MUC2. For each carrier and a matched set of non-carriers, we will calculate a UC PRS using established common variant associations from pre-2026 GWAS. We will employ logistic regression models with an interaction term between MUC2 rare variant carrier status and PRS (as a continuous variable) to test for effect modification on UC diagnosis and severity phenotypes, adjusting for relevant covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for integrating rare and common variant risk in a complex disease, moving beyond additive models. It will offer mechanistic insight into the variable expressivity of rare variants and could refine personalized risk prediction by stratifying rare variant carriers based on their common variant background.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard model where rare variants are treated as primary drivers, instead testing the common variant background as a key modulator of rare variant effects, a specific hypothesis not yet systematically tested for UC and MUC2.

proposals.title: Rare Variant Burden Analysis Across the Ulcerative Colitis Phenotypic Spectrum: From Disease Susceptibility to Severity and Treatment Response
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and rare variant analyses for ulcerative colitis (UC) have largely relied on a binary case-control framework, identifying susceptibility loci but explaining limited phenotypic heterogeneity. This approach fails to capture how rare genetic variants influence disease course, severity, and therapeutic outcomes, which are critical for precision medicine. Integrating deep phenotypic data from large biobanks with sequencing data offers a novel opportunity to move beyond simple case status.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the aggregate burden of rare, predicted deleterious variants in specific gene sets (e.g., immune-related pathways) is associated not only with UC susceptibility but also with quantitative measures of disease severity, progression, and differential response to first-line therapies like anti-TNF agents.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-exome/genome sequencing data from ~50,000 UK Biobank participants, leveraging deep-linked electronic health records to define UC sub-phenotypes (e.g., Montreal classification, hospitalization history, need for colectomy) and treatment response proxies. Using optimized rare variant burden tests (e.g., SKAT-O, STAAR) and gene-set enrichment analyses, we will test associations between rare variant burden (MAF<0.01) in pre-defined pathways and continuous/ordinal phenotypic scores. We will control for population structure and common variant polygenic risk scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the specific genetic architecture of UC clinical heterogeneity, identifying rare variant sets that predict severe disease course or treatment resistance. It will provide a methodological framework for moving rare variant analysis into quantitative trait spaces in complex diseases, directly informing risk stratification and trial design.
proposals.novelty: This study shifts the focus of rare variant analysis from disease susceptibility to mechanistic drivers of clinical heterogeneity within an established disease, a largely unexplored application pre-2026. It systematically integrates biobank-scale sequencing with rich, longitudinal phenotypic data beyond diagnostic codes.

proposals.title: Trans-ethnic Replication and Burden Analysis of Rare MUC2 Variants in Ulcerative Colitis
proposals.motivation: Ulcerative Colitis (UC) is a complex inflammatory bowel disease with a significant genetic component. Recent studies in Dutch and German cohorts have implicated rare, damaging variants in the MUC2 gene, which encodes the critical intestinal mucin MUC2, in UC susceptibility. However, the generalizability of these findings across diverse ancestries remains unknown, limiting our understanding of their global relevance and potential for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, predicted loss-of-function (pLoF) and damaging missense variants in MUC2 are associated with increased risk of Ulcerative Colitis across multiple, ancestrally diverse populations, and that this association is independent of common variant signals.
proposals.method: We will perform a rare variant burden analysis using whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing data from large, independent cohorts (e.g., the UK Biobank, the All of Us Research Program, and international IBD consortia). Cases and controls will be rigorously phenotyped. We will aggregate rare variants (MAF < 0.1%) in MUC2 by functional consequence (pLoF, damaging missense) using the Sequence Kernel Association Test (SKAT-O) and burden tests, implemented in tools like REGENIE or SAIGE-GENE. Analyses will be stratified by major ancestry groups (e.g., European, East Asian, African) to assess trans-ethnic consistency, with covariates for population structure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive evidence on the generalizability of MUC2 rare variant associations in UC, either validating it as a pan-ancestry risk gene or revealing population-specific effects. It will clarify the role of mucin barrier dysfunction in UC pathogenesis. The findings will inform the potential of MUC2 as a target for stratified risk prediction or therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While MUC2 rare variants have been linked to UC in specific European populations, this study is the first systematic, large-scale effort to replicate and evaluate these associations across major global ancestries, moving beyond initial discovery to robust validation in a pre-2026 framework.

proposals.title: Systematic Resequencing of Novel Candidate Genes from Cross-Species GWAS to Elucidate Rare Variant Architecture in Complex Developmental Disorders
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous loci associated with complex developmental disorders and disease risk, but the specific causal genes and variants, particularly rare coding mutations, remain largely uncharacterized. This 'missing heritability' gap limits biological understanding and clinical translation. Expanding targeted resequencing beyond the usual suspects to novel candidates from cross-species analyses and fine-mapped GWAS signals is a critical next step to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted resequencing of a broad panel of novel candidate genes, nominated by human GWAS fine-mapping and phenotypic analogies in model organisms, will identify an enriched burden of rare, likely deleterious coding variants associated with specific developmental disorders and disease risk in patients.
proposals.method: We will design a custom hybrid-capture panel targeting ~500 genes, including novel candidates from fine-mapped GWAS loci for disorders like Crohn's disease and established developmental disease genes. We will perform deep sequencing (mean coverage >100x) on 5,000 well-phenotyped patients with related developmental disorders and 5,000 matched controls from existing biobank resources (e.g., UK Biobank). Variant calling will use GATK best practices, and association testing for gene-based burden and single-variant effects will be performed using optimized algorithms like SKAT-O and REGENIE, adjusting for population structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly nominate new risk genes and specific pathogenic rare variants for complex developmental diseases, moving beyond association signals to causal mechanisms. It will generate a publicly available dataset of rare coding variation in candidate genes, serving as a resource for the community and informing functional studies and potential therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, large-scale integration of cross-species phenotypic data with human GWAS to select novel candidate genes for resequencing, moving beyond the standard practice of sequencing only genes in known pathways or with the strongest prior evidence.

proposals.title: Gene-based burden and variance component tests reveal the contribution of rare coding variation to Ulcerative Colitis risk and phenotypic heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Ulcerative Colitis (UC) is a complex, immune-mediated disorder with a significant but incompletely characterized genetic component. While genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified over 200 common variant loci, they explain only a fraction of heritability, and the role of rare, penetrant coding variants—akin to those in monogenic forms of inflammatory bowel disease—remains poorly understood in the broader UC population. Applying sophisticated burden tests, developed for rare diseases, to large-scale UC sequencing data can bridge this gap, identifying risk genes and informing biological mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aggregated rare, predicted-damaging coding variants within specific genes and pathways, detectable via gene-based burden tests, contribute significantly to UC susceptibility and clinical subphenotypes, complementing common variant risk.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing data from large UC case-control cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, IBD sequencing consortia). We will implement established gene-based burden tests (e.g., SKAT, SKAT-O, Burden tests) and variance component models to aggregate rare (MAF<0.01) predicted-loss-of-function and deleterious missense variants per gene. Analyses will be stratified by clinical features (e.g., age of onset, disease extent) and integrated with common variant polygenic risk scores to assess independent and interactive effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel UC risk genes driven by rare variants, refine biological pathways implicated in disease pathogenesis, and provide a framework for genetic subtyping based on combined rare and common variant architecture. It will directly extend the successful analytical paradigm from monogenic disorders to a complex disease, offering a more complete genetic model of UC.
proposals.novelty: While burden tests are standard in monogenic disease genetics, their systematic application to UC sequencing data to dissect both risk and heterogeneity is novel, moving beyond the dominant common-variant paradigm to integrate a spectrum of genetic effects.

proposals.title: A Robust Bayesian Framework for Quantifying Variant Pathogenicity from Familial Co-segregation Data in Complex Diseases
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genomic studies have identified numerous rare variants associated with disease risk, but distinguishing truly pathogenic mutations from benign rare variants remains a critical bottleneck. Familial co-segregation analysis is a cornerstone of clinical variant interpretation, yet existing statistical models are often simplistic, fail to account for complex disease etiologies like incomplete penetrance and polygenic background, and lack robust integration with population-scale biobank data. This limits the reliable translation of genomic discoveries into clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a statistical model incorporating disease-specific genetic architecture parameters (e.g., penetrance, mode of inheritance, polygenic risk score distributions) and pedigree uncertainty will generate more accurate, calibrated evidence scores for variant pathogenicity from family data than current methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a flexible Bayesian framework where the likelihood of observed disease phenotypes in a pedigree is computed under competing hypotheses (variant causative vs. not). The model will integrate: 1) prior probabilities informed by variant frequency and constraint from gnomAD, 2) penetrance functions conditioned on polygenic risk scores (PRS) derived from biobank-wide association studies (e.g., UK Biobank), and 3) Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to handle uncertainty in genotyping and pedigree structures. We will validate the model using simulated families and real data from cohorts with known monogenic subsets of complex diseases (e.g., *BRCA1* in breast cancer, high-risk IBD variants).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a statistically rigorous, open-source tool for co-segregation analysis that explicitly models the complexities of common diseases. It will improve the classification of Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS) and strengthen the evidence for gene-disease relationships, directly aiding clinical diagnostics and the interpretation of findings from large sequencing studies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing co-segregation tools designed for Mendelian disorders, our model uniquely incorporates polygenic risk as a modifier of penetrance within families, creating a bridge between rare variant discovery in biobanks and familial evidence for pathogenicity.

proposals.title: Functional Dissection of High-Confidence Synaptic and Chromatin Risk Genes Implicated in Neuropsychiatric and Developmental Disorders
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genomic studies, such as the UK Biobank and large-scale sequencing consortia, have identified numerous genes associated with neuropsychiatric and developmental disorders, but the functional consequences of the implicated mutations remain largely uncharacterized. Moving from statistical association to mechanistic understanding is a critical bottleneck for translating genetic discoveries into biological insights and potential therapeutic targets. This is particularly urgent for genes involved in synaptic function and chromatin regulation, key pathways disrupted in these complex diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare, damaging mutations in the 107 high-confidence synaptic and chromatin risk genes disrupt specific molecular and cellular functions, including neuronal differentiation, synaptic transmission, and chromatin accessibility, thereby conferring disease risk.
proposals.method: We will employ a tiered functional validation strategy. First, in vitro assays using CRISPR/Cas9-engineered human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived neurons will assess the impact of prioritized mutations on neuronal morphology, synaptic density (via immunocytochemistry), and electrophysiological properties (patch-clamp). Second, for chromatin-regulating genes, we will perform ATAC-seq and RNA-seq on mutant neuronal lines to quantify changes in chromatin accessibility and gene expression. Third, select high-impact genes will be validated in vivo using zebrafish models, leveraging CRISPR mutagenesis to assay developmental phenotypes and neuronal circuit function.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a functional atlas linking specific genetic variants in high-confidence risk genes to quantifiable cellular and molecular phenotypes. It will prioritize a subset of genes and variants for deeper mechanistic study and drug discovery efforts. The data will provide essential biological validation for genome-wide association and sequencing studies, moving the field beyond correlation towards causation.
proposals.novelty: While many studies list candidate genes, this proposal systematically applies a multi-assay platform—spanning human cellular models and in vivo vertebrate validation—specifically to a curated set of synaptic and chromatin genes derived from the latest pre-2026 biobank-scale analyses, integrating electrophysiology and epigenomic profiling in a unified pipeline.

proposals.title: A deep learning framework for predicting the pathogenicity of missense variants in synaptic and chromatin genes using large-scale biobank genomic and metabolomic data
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of variant pathogenicity is critical for interpreting genetic findings in complex neurodevelopmental and psychiatric disorders, where synaptic and chromatin genes are frequently implicated. Pre-2026 tools like AlphaMissense and EVE show promise but are often trained on evolutionary data or limited clinical labels, lacking integration with rich, population-scale biomarker and disease phenotype data available in modern biobanks. This gap limits their utility for pinpointing disease-relevant variants in specific biological pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep neural network trained on the integrated genomic, metabolomic, and clinical data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) will outperform existing methods in predicting the functional impact and disease association of rare missense variants in synaptic and chromatin regulatory genes.
proposals.method: We will train a multi-modal deep learning model, likely a hybrid architecture combining convolutional layers for processing sequence context and protein features with dense layers for integrating variant-level features and individual-level metabolomic profiles from the UK Biobank NMR atlas. The model will be trained to predict a composite label derived from ClinVar annotations and disease associations from biobank phenome-wide scans, using a cohort of ~500,000 individuals. We will benchmark against state-of-the-art pre-2026 predictors (e.g., AlphaMissense, CADD) using held-out gene sets and validation in independent cohorts for disorders like Crohn's disease.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, clinically-informed predictor for missense variants in two high-impact gene classes, directly linking variant effect to intermediate molecular phenotypes (metabolomics) and disease risk. It will provide a framework for leveraging biobank data to move beyond evolutionary conservation towards pathophysiology-aware variant interpretation, aiding in the prioritization of causal mutations in genetic studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of population-scale individual-level metabolomic data and disease diagnoses as training signals for variant effect prediction, moving beyond sequence-based or conservation-based models to a more integrated, systems-level approach grounded in human phenotypic data.

proposals.title: Resolving the Functional Architecture of GWAS Loci: A Long-Read Sequencing Study to Identify Rare Structural Variants as Causal Drivers of Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of GWAS in identifying thousands of loci associated with complex diseases, a substantial fraction of heritability remains unexplained, and the functional variants within associated loci are often not the common tag SNPs. Rare structural variants (SVs), including complex insertions, deletions, and rearrangements, are poorly captured by SNP arrays and short-read sequencing, representing a major class of potentially high-impact, hidden genetic variation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within established GWAS loci for disorders like Crohn's disease, rare structural and complex variants, resolved only by deep-coverage long-read sequencing, are the primary functional drivers of phenotypic risk and a key source of missing heritability.
proposals.method: We will perform deep-coverage (≥30X) PacBio HiFi or Oxford Nanopore sequencing on a targeted cohort of 1,000 individuals with extreme phenotypes from the UK Biobank, focusing on the top 50 loci associated with Crohn's disease and other immune-mediated disorders. We will use established SV callers (e.g., Sniffles2, cuteSV) and graph-based genotyping methods (e.g., Paragraph) to comprehensively catalog SVs. Association testing for rare SVs (MAF<0.01) will be performed using burden and SKAT-O tests, integrating with existing common SNP data to assess conditional independence and effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution map of rare SVs within GWAS loci for major diseases, directly testing their contribution to risk. It will move beyond statistical association to nominate specific, resolvable candidate causal variants for functional validation, addressing a core limitation in post-GWAS biology.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its systematic application of deep-coverage long-read sequencing as a targeted 'profiling' tool within GWAS loci, rather than for genome-wide discovery, analogous to seismic imaging, to explicitly test if unresolved structural variation underlies common disease associations.

proposals.title: Deep Resequencing of Common Disease Loci to Resolve Missing Heritability Through Rare Variant Discovery
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of common variant loci associated with complex diseases, yet these variants often explain only a fraction of estimated heritability, a persistent gap known as the 'missing heritability' problem. Recent large-scale sequencing efforts, such as those for Crohn's disease, suggest rare coding variants within GWAS loci can have larger effect sizes, but a systematic, deep-resequencing investigation across multiple common diseases is lacking. This project directly addresses the central challenge of whether rare variants are a major source of the unexplained heritability in known risk loci.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep resequencing of genomic regions encompassing GWAS-identified common variant signals for major diseases will reveal an enrichment of rare, penetrant coding and regulatory variants that account for a significant portion of the phenotypic variance unexplained by common SNPs.
proposals.method: We will perform targeted deep sequencing (coverage >100x) of all exons, splice sites, and conserved non-coding regions within linkage disequilibrium blocks of top GWAS loci for three diseases (selected from UK Biobank atlas data: e.g., coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes, inflammatory bowel disease). Using data from ~50,000 pre-existing biobank samples with rich phenotyping, we will apply variant calling (GATK best practices) and rare variant association testing using burden and sequence kernel association tests (SKAT-O). Analyses will be stratified by variant functional annotation (using tools like ANNOVAR and CADD) and integrated with matched metabolomic data to identify putative mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive assessment of the contribution of rare variants in GWAS loci to common disease risk, moving the field beyond the common disease-common variant paradigm. It will generate a publicly available catalog of high-impact rare variants for functional follow-up and inform the design of more comprehensive genetic risk scores that incorporate both common and rare variation.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have sequenced cases for single diseases, our approach leverages the massive, deeply phenotyped UK Biobank cohort to perform a systematic, multi-trait analysis, integrating sequencing with biomarker data to dissect the architecture of heritability within known loci rather than genome-wide.

proposals.title: Genetic Subtyping of Type 2 Diabetes Cases Reveals Distinct Etiological Pathways and Improves Polygenic Risk Prediction
proposals.motivation: Type 2 diabetes (T2D) is a highly prevalent, complex disease with substantial clinical heterogeneity, suggesting it may represent a collection of distinct disorders with different underlying genetic architectures. Standard genome-wide association studies (GWAS) treat all cases as a single entity, potentially masking subtype-specific genetic risk factors and hindering the development of precise prevention and treatment strategies. The availability of large-scale biobank data with genomic and metabolomic profiling, as demonstrated in recent large-scale studies, now enables a systematic investigation of this heterogeneity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that T2D cases can be stratified into genetically distinct subtypes based on their polygenic risk score profiles and associated biomarker signatures, and that these subtypes will exhibit different associations with rare coding variants, developmental disorders, and clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will apply unsupervised clustering algorithms (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models, K-means) to T2D cases from the UK Biobank using multi-dimensional features derived from pre-2026 knowledge: (1) polygenic risk scores for T2D and its related metabolic traits, (2) plasma NMR metabolomic biomarker profiles, and (3) electronic health record-derived comorbidities. We will then perform GWAS within each identified genetic subtype to discover subtype-specific common variants and test for enrichment of rare protein-truncating mutations in genes associated with developmental disorders and monogenic diabetes.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the genetic architecture of T2D subtypes, directly linking heterogeneous disease mechanisms to specific genetic risk profiles. It will generate subtype-specific polygenic scores that improve disease prediction and risk stratification beyond current models. The findings will provide a framework for etiological classification that can guide targeted therapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified genetic loci for T2D, this proposal moves beyond bulk analysis to perform an integrated, data-driven genetic subtyping of cases themselves, a critical step towards precision medicine. The integration of common variant polygenic scores with rare variant burden testing and metabolomic data for subtyping represents a novel, multi-modal approach to disease deconstruction.

proposals.title: A Two-Stage Causal Pipeline to Identify and Prospectively Validate Gene-Environment Interactions for Common Diseases
proposals.motivation: Agnostic searches for gene-environment interactions (GxE) in biobanks have high false-discovery rates, hindering translation to public health. Mendelian Randomization (MR) offers a causal framework for hypothesis generation but has not been systematically used to prioritize GxE for prospective testing. Validating replicable, modifiable interactions is crucial for developing stratified prevention strategies for common diseases like Crohn's disease, where both genetic risk and environmental factors are implicated.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage causal pipeline, using MR for hypothesis generation followed by prospective cohort validation, can identify robust, replicable GxE interactions on the additive scale, with objective biomarkers providing mechanistic insight into disease pathways.
proposals.method: Stage 1: In UK Biobank data, we will apply two-sample MR using genetic instruments for modifiable exposures (e.g., plasma NMR biomarkers) to test for interaction effects with polygenic risk scores (PRS) for target diseases. Stage 2: Top-prioritized interactions will be tested in a pre-registered, prospective cohort study (n>50,000) with deep phenotyping. We will employ additive interaction models and use objective exposure biomarkers (e.g., metabolomic profiles) and intermediate phenotypes (e.g., immune markers) to validate causal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, low false-discovery-rate pipeline for GxE discovery, moving beyond agnostic searches. It will produce a shortlist of prospectively confirmed, modifiable environmental targets for disease prevention in genetically susceptible subgroups, directly informing precision public health.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of MR as a formal hypothesis-generating tool for GxE, followed by prospective validation with objective biomarkers, creates a principled causal inference pipeline distinct from standard one-stage, agnostic interaction testing in biobanks.

proposals.title: Haplotype-Specific Gene-Environment Interaction Networks Underlying Metabolic Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: Large-scale biobank studies have identified numerous genetic variants and environmental factors associated with complex diseases, yet the molecular mechanisms underlying gene-environment interactions (GxE) remain opaque. Common risk variants often reside in non-coding regions on specific haplotype backgrounds, suggesting their effects may be contingent on both the local genomic context and environmental exposures. Bridging epidemiological GxE findings with causal regulatory circuits is a critical unmet need to move from association to mechanism.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, common haplotypes harboring disease-associated variants confer environmental sensitivity by altering cis-regulatory element activity (e.g., enhancer function) in response to metabolic or dietary cues, thereby modulating disease-relevant gene expression.
proposals.method: We will integrate phased genotype data from biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) with functional genomic annotations (eQTLs, chromatin accessibility/activity from projects like ENCODE and GTEx) to define haplotype-resolved regulatory landscapes. For loci implicated in GxE studies for metabolic traits, we will test for haplotype-environment interactions on molecular phenotypes (e.g., allele-specific expression in relevant tissues) using statistical models. Causal regulatory variants and their interacting environmental factors will be identified by combining fine-mapping (e.g., using SuSiE) with in silico perturbation of transcription factor binding motifs altered by the risk haplotype.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a functional and haplotype-aware framework for interpreting GxE, moving beyond single-variant models. It will deliver a catalog of environmentally responsive regulatory haplotypes for metabolic diseases, offering testable hypotheses for experimental validation and highlighting potential targets for personalized prevention strategies based on both genetic background and environment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GxE studies focused on epidemiological interactions or single-variant functional assays, this approach systematically integrates phased haplotypes, environmental data, and multi-omic functional annotations to resolve the specific cis-regulatory architectures that mediate environmental sensitivity.

proposals.title: Dissecting the genetic architecture of Crohn's disease: A comprehensive analysis of gene-gene and gene-environment interactions in the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Despite the identification of numerous common and rare genetic variants associated with Crohn's disease susceptibility, a substantial portion of disease heritability and phenotypic variance remains unexplained. This 'missing heritability' is hypothesized to arise from complex interactions between genetic loci (epistasis) and between genetic risk factors and modifiable environmental exposures, such as diet and obesity. Understanding these interactions is crucial for improving risk prediction models and elucidating biological pathways for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of common and rare genetic variants, identified through large-scale sequencing, interact synergistically with each other and with environmental factors to significantly modulate Crohn's disease risk beyond additive genetic effects.
proposals.method: We will leverage the UK Biobank cohort, integrating genomic data from over 100,000 sequenced individuals (including known Crohn's-associated variants) with extensive phenotypic data, NMR metabolomic biomarkers, and environmental questionnaires. We will employ a two-stage analytical approach: first, using statistical methods like Multifactor Dimensionality Reduction (MDR) and regression-based interaction tests to screen for significant gene-gene pairs. Second, we will apply machine learning models, specifically gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) and regularized regression (LASSO), to model higher-order interactions and integrate environmental covariates (e.g., BMI, dietary scores) to build a comprehensive risk prediction model.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed map of significant epistatic networks and gene-environment interactions specific to Crohn's disease, moving beyond single-variant associations. It will deliver an improved polygenic risk score that incorporates interaction terms, enhancing predictive accuracy. The findings will highlight modifiable environmental risk factors that are most pertinent for individuals with specific genetic backgrounds, informing personalized prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has catalogued individual risk variants, this proposal systematically tests for interactions between the full spectrum of common and rare variants implicated in Crohn's, directly integrating this with high-dimensional biomarker and environmental data from the same large cohort, a comprehensive approach not yet fully realized in pre-2026 studies.

proposals.title: The Metabolic Trait Genetic Atlas: A Common Coordinate Framework for Integrating Genetic Associations Across Cardiometabolic Diseases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for related metabolic traits like type 2 diabetes, obesity, and dyslipidemia are conducted in large, siloed consortia. This fragmentation hinders the systematic identification of shared versus trait-specific genetic architectures and regulatory modules, limiting our understanding of the interconnected causal pathways underlying metabolic disease. A unified framework is needed to move beyond simple cross-trait comparisons and enable true integrative analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a standardized, multi-dimensional reference framework for metabolic trait genetics will reveal coherent patterns of shared genetic regulation, distinguish disease-specific loci, and map genetic signals to specific cellular and pathway contexts more effectively than siloed analyses.
proposals.method: We will integrate summary statistics from major pre-2026 metabolic disease consortia (e.g., DIAGRAM, GIANT, MAGIC) and UK Biobank biomarker studies. Using a combination of genetic correlation (LD Score Regression), multi-trait conditional and colocalization analysis (COLOC, eCAVIAR), and pathway enrichment, we will map associated loci onto a unified coordinate system. This system will be defined by axes of genomic position, cell-type-specific chromatin accessibility (from pre-2026 single-cell ATAC-seq data), and curated biological pathways (e.g., KEGG, Reactome). We will develop a queryable database and visualization interface analogous to neuroimaging atlases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrative genetic atlas for cardiometabolic traits, providing a public resource for mapping new associations. It will systematically identify pleiotropic genetic hubs and trait-specific signals, offering a clearer roadmap for functional validation and highlighting potential therapeutic targets with effects across multiple disease states.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the common coordinate framework concept from neuroimaging to genetic data integration, creating a biologically anchored reference space for systematic querying rather than performing a series of pairwise comparisons. This approach is distinct from existing genetic correlation or meta-analysis methods.

proposals.title: Cell-type-resolved expression quantitative trait locus mapping in metabolically relevant tissues reveals cellular contexts for type 2 diabetes genetic risk
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of loci associated with type 2 diabetes (T2D), but the majority reside in non-coding regions, implicating regulatory dysfunction. While bulk tissue eQTL studies in pancreas, liver, and adipose tissue have been performed, they average signals across heterogeneous cell populations, obscuring the specific cell types (e.g., pancreatic beta cells, hepatocytes) in which risk variants alter gene expression to drive disease susceptibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying computational cell-type deconvolution to existing bulk tissue eQTL datasets will reveal a significant enrichment of T2D GWAS risk variants for cell-type-specific regulatory effects, particularly in key metabolic cell populations, thereby pinpointing the precise cellular contexts of genetic risk.
proposals.method: We will apply state-of-the-art deconvolution algorithms, specifically CIBERSORTx and MuSiC, to bulk RNA-seq data from major T2D-relevant eQTL resources (e.g., GTEx, TIGER, and islet-specific datasets). Using matched single-cell RNA-seq reference profiles for pancreatic islets, adipose, and liver, we will estimate cell-type proportions for each bulk sample and perform eQTL mapping conditioned on these proportions to identify cell-type-interaction eQTLs (ct-eQTLs). We will then integrate these ct-eQTLs with T2D GWAS summary statistics using colocalization and transcriptome-wide association study (TWAS) frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive atlas of cell-type-specific regulatory effects for T2D-relevant tissues, directly linking non-coding risk variants to candidate causal genes and effector cell types. It will prioritize cell populations for functional follow-up and provide a mechanistic, cellular-resolution model of T2D genetic architecture, moving beyond tissue-level associations.
proposals.novelty: While cell-type deconvolution for eQTL mapping has been pioneered in neuropsychiatry (e.g., for schizophrenia), its systematic application to dissect the cellular basis of T2D genetic risk represents a novel methodological transfer, addressing a key bottleneck in diabetes genomics where key cell types are often scarce and difficult to profile directly at scale.

proposals.title: Integrating Multi-Tissue eQTL Maps with Polygenic Risk Scores to Decipher Causal Genes and Regulatory Mechanisms for Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of loci associated with Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) risk, but the causal genes and biological mechanisms at most loci remain unknown. This gap hinders the translation of genetic findings into actionable biological insights or therapeutic targets. Expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) mapping provides a powerful tool to link genetic variants to gene expression, yet its systematic application to T2D across all relevant metabolic and endocrine tissues is incomplete.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating T2D GWAS summary statistics with multi-tissue eQTL maps will identify specific, high-confidence causal genes whose expression is modulated by risk variants, and that these genes will converge on dysregulated biological pathways in key disease-relevant tissues like pancreas, liver, adipose, and skeletal muscle.
proposals.method: We will perform a transcriptome-wide colocalization analysis using state-of-the-art methods (e.g., COLOC, SMR) to integrate large-scale T2D GWAS data (from consortia like DIAGRAM) with eQTL data from comprehensive public resources (GTEx v8, eQTLGen, and T2D-relevant tissue-specific studies). We will prioritize genes with strong colocalization evidence (PP4 > 0.8) and validate findings using Mendelian Randomization. Fine-mapping of credible sets will be conducted using SuSiE to identify likely causal variants. Pathway and network analyses (using DEPICT or MAGMA) will be performed on the prioritized gene set.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a prioritized list of likely causal genes for T2D, moving beyond statistical associations to mechanistic hypotheses. It will delineate the tissue-specific regulatory architecture of T2D risk and identify key dysregulated biological pathways. The results will provide a foundational resource for designing functional validation experiments and nominating potential targets for drug development.
proposals.novelty: While eQTL colocalization is established, this proposal's novelty lies in its systematic, multi-tissue integration focused specifically on T2D, leveraging the latest and largest pre-2026 GWAS and eQTL datasets to perform robust fine-mapping and pathway analysis, thereby moving from variant-to-gene mapping to a systems-level understanding of disease etiology.

proposals.title: A Formal Econometric Framework for Instrument Validation in Mendelian Randomization Studies of Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Mendelian randomization (MR) has become a cornerstone for causal inference in epidemiology, yet its application to complex diseases like Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is often limited by pleiotropy and weak instrument bias. Pre-2026, many MR studies in T2D rely on genetic variants from large-scale GWAS but lack rigorous, standardized validation of the core instrumental variable (IV) assumptions, potentially leading to spurious causal claims. This gap hinders the translation of genetic discoveries into credible biological and clinical insights.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formally adapting and applying the stringent instrument validation framework from econometrics—specifically testing for relevance, independence, and the exclusion restriction—will yield more robust and less biased causal estimates for modifiable risk factors in T2D compared to standard MR practices.
proposals.method: We will analyze genetic and phenotypic data from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) to construct MR studies for T2D risk factors like BMI, lipid levels, and liver enzymes. For each candidate genetic instrument (individual SNPs or polygenic scores), we will implement econometric validation tests: (1) F-statistics for weak instrument testing (relevance), (2) covariate balance tests using observed confounders (independence), and (3) systematic sensitivity analyses, including MR-Egger, weighted median estimator, and leave-one-out analyses, to assess violation of the exclusion restriction (pleiotropy). We will compare causal estimates from instruments passing this formal validation to those from standard MR approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, rigorous protocol for instrument selection and validation in T2D MR studies, directly addressing concerns about pleiotropy. It will deliver a more credible set of causal relationships between metabolic traits and T2D, informing primary prevention strategies. The framework will serve as a model for causal inference in other complex diseases studied by the researcher, such as Crohn's disease.
proposals.novelty: While MR is conceptually based on IV principles, the explicit translation of formal econometric testing procedures—designed for observational social science data—into the genetic epidemiology workflow for T2D is novel. It moves beyond common MR sensitivity analyses by enforcing a pre-specified, sequential validation regimen mirroring econometric best practices for causal inference.

proposals.title: Causal Effects of Plasma Metabolites on Type 2 Diabetes Risk: A Multi-Biobank Mendelian Randomization Study
proposals.motivation: Type 2 diabetes (T2D) is a major global health burden with complex, incompletely understood etiology. Observational studies link numerous circulating metabolites to T2D risk, but these associations may be confounded or reversed by disease processes. Establishing causal direction is critical for identifying modifiable risk factors and preventive targets. Large-scale biobanks with genomic and metabolomic data, like the UK Biobank, now enable powerful causal inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific plasma metabolites, identified via nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, have a causal, modifiable influence on T2D risk, distinct from the effects of known clinical risk factors like BMI.
proposals.method: We will perform a two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) analysis using genetic variants as instrumental variables. First, we will obtain genetic associations for hundreds of NMR-quantified plasma metabolites from the Atlas of Plasma NMR Biomarkers (n=118,461 UK Biobank). Second, we will obtain genetic associations for T2D from large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and biobank cohorts (e.g., >400,000 participants). We will apply inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary analysis, supplemented by sensitivity methods (MR-Egger, weighted median) to test for and correct pleiotropy. Significant metabolites will be further analyzed using multivariable MR to adjust for genetic correlations with BMI and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate a prioritized set of plasma metabolites with putative causal roles in T2D pathogenesis, moving beyond association to causation. It will directly inform the development of novel biomarkers for risk stratification and highlight potential metabolic pathways for therapeutic intervention. The multi-biobank framework ensures robustness and generalizability of the findings.
proposals.novelty: While prior MR studies have examined individual metabolites, this proposal leverages the unprecedented scale and resolution of the newly available plasma NMR atlas in the UK Biobank to systematically screen the causal role of the human metabolome in T2D, while rigorously accounting for the confounding effect of body size through multivariable MR.

proposals.title: Single-cell functional genomics of pancreatic beta cell subtypes reveals cell-type-specific mechanisms of type 2 diabetes risk variants
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of loci associated with type 2 diabetes (T2D) risk, but the causal genes, variants, and cellular mechanisms remain largely unknown. The pancreatic islet, particularly insulin-producing beta cells, is a key site of action, but it is a heterogeneous population. Pre-2026 bulk genomic and metabolomic studies, like those in large biobanks, lack the resolution to link genetic risk to specific, functionally distinct beta cell subtypes, hindering mechanistic understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that T2D-associated genetic variants exert their effects by dysregulating gene expression and functional pathways in specific, transcriptionally defined beta cell subtypes, and that these subtype-specific disruptions are detectable through integrated single-cell multi-omics.
proposals.method: We will perform single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) and single-nucleus ATAC-seq (snATAC-seq) on pancreatic islets from ~50 human donors with known T2D genotype data. Using Seurat v4 for integrated clustering and cell-type annotation, we will identify beta cell subtypes. We will map T2D GWAS variants to subtype-specific cis-regulatory elements (CREs) via snATAC-seq peaks and perform single-cell expression quantitative trait locus (sc-eQTL) analysis using TensorQTL to identify variant-to-gene links. Functional validation of top candidate variants will be conducted in stem-cell-derived beta-like cells using CRISPR interference (CRISPRi).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of genetic regulatory effects across human beta cell subtypes, directly linking non-coding T2D risk variants to their target genes and dysregulated pathways in specific cellular contexts. It will move beyond association to reveal the cell-type-specific functional mechanisms underlying genetic risk, providing new targets for subtype-selective therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified T2D genes and variants in bulk tissues, this proposal uniquely applies single-cell multi-omics and sc-eQTL mapping specifically to dissect human pancreatic beta cell heterogeneity, a critical pre-2026 knowledge gap, to pinpoint where and how genetic risk manifests at cellular resolution.

proposals.title: Resolving Causal Variants from GWAS Signals: A Multi-Omic Integration Framework for Inflammatory Bowel Disease
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of SNPs associated with complex diseases, but the vast majority are non-coding and in linkage disequilibrium (LD) with many other variants, obscuring the true causal variants. This impedes biological understanding and the translation of genetic findings into mechanistic insights and therapeutic targets, particularly for disorders like Crohn's disease where numerous susceptibility loci have been mapped but few causal variants are definitively known.
proposals.hypothesis: For a significant proportion of GWAS-identified loci for Crohn's disease, the lead SNP or a highly correlated variant directly alters gene regulatory function (e.g., transcription factor binding, enhancer activity) or protein structure, and this functional impact can be systematically distinguished from passive LD through integrated genomic analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a statistical and computational pipeline focusing on high-confidence Crohn's disease loci from large-scale sequencing studies. For each locus, we will integrate fine-mapping results (using methods like SuSiE) with functional genomic annotations (e.g., ATAC-seq, H3K27ac ChIP-seq from disease-relevant immune cells), expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) data, and deep mutational scanning predictions of variant effect. A Bayesian probabilistic framework will be employed to weight and combine this evidence to assign a posterior probability of causality to each variant in the LD block.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a prioritized, high-confidence set of putative causal variants for Crohn's disease, moving beyond association signals to specific testable hypotheses about disease mechanisms. It will establish a generalizable framework for causal variant resolution that combines state-of-the-art statistical genetics with functional genomics, directly addressing a major bottleneck in post-GWAS biology.
proposals.novelty: While fine-mapping and functional annotation are established, our approach uniquely integrates deep mutational scanning predictions of non-coding variant impact and cell-type-specific regulatory data within a unified Bayesian model specifically tailored for loci identified through large-scale biobank sequencing, offering a more systematic and quantitative assessment of causality.

proposals.title: Deep Metabolic Phenotyping of the FTO Locus Reveals Distinct Subtypes of Cardiometabolic Risk
proposals.motivation: The FTO locus is a major genetic risk factor for obesity and type 2 diabetes, but its biological mechanisms remain incompletely understood. The association is complex and heterogeneous, suggesting that the FTO variant may influence diverse metabolic pathways, not just overall adiposity. Resolving this heterogeneity is crucial for developing targeted interventions and understanding individual disease risk trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the FTO risk variant is associated with distinct, quantifiable metabolic sub-phenotypes, identifiable through high-resolution NMR metabolomics, which mediate differential risks for specific cardiometabolic disorders beyond aggregate BMI.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-existing deep metabolic profiling data from the UK Biobank (e.g., the Atlas of plasma NMR biomarkers) for ~120,000 individuals. Using a two-stage approach, we will first perform a multivariate association analysis between the lead FTO variant and the full panel of NMR biomarkers, adjusting for BMI. We will then apply unsupervised clustering (e.g., k-means or Gaussian Mixture Models) on the most strongly associated biomarkers to identify metabolic subtypes. Finally, we will test these subtypes for differential associations with incident disease endpoints using Cox regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the coarse phenotype of BMI to define precise metabolic mechanisms through which FTO exerts its effects. It will provide a novel, biologically grounded stratification of FTO-associated risk, identifying subgroups of carriers with higher susceptibility to specific disorders like coronary artery disease or hepatic steatosis. This could inform more personalized risk prediction and mechanistic studies.
proposals.novelty: While FTO's link to obesity is well-known, applying deep, multivariate metabolic phenotyping to dissect its heterogeneous effects at a population scale is novel. This approach moves from a single disease association to mapping a variant onto a network of intermediate biochemical pathways, a strategy not yet comprehensively applied to this locus.

proposals.title: Discovery and Characterization of Rare, High-Effect FTO Variants for Metabolic Disease Risk in Mega-Cohort Genomic Data
proposals.motivation: Common variants in the FTO gene are robustly associated with obesity and type 2 diabetes, but they explain only a fraction of heritability. Pre-2026 studies suggest that rare, penetrant variants may exist within established risk loci but remain undetected due to insufficient statistical power in cohorts of hundreds of thousands. Identifying such variants is critical for understanding the full allelic architecture of disease and for pinpointing causal mechanisms with larger effect sizes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by scaling whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing to multi-million participant mega-cohorts, we will identify rare (MAF < 0.1%), coding and non-coding variants in the FTO locus that have substantially larger effect sizes on metabolic traits and disease risk than common FTO variants.
proposals.method: We will perform a meta-analysis of FTO-region variants across the UK Biobank, All of Us, and other large-scale biobanks, targeting a combined sample size exceeding 1.5 million individuals with genomic and phenotypic data. We will use established rare-variant association testing frameworks, specifically gene-based burden and SKAT-O tests, focusing on the FTO locus and its regulatory regions. Associations will be tested against a panel of quantitative metabolic biomarkers (e.g., NMR-derived lipid profiles, glycated hemoglobin) and disease endpoints (obesity, T2D), adjusting for common FTO SNP effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the spectrum of rare variation in a canonical disease locus, potentially revealing novel biological pathways with outsized effects on metabolic health. It will provide a model for leveraging mega-cohorts to dissect the 'missing heritability' within known GWAS loci, moving from statistical association to the identification of likely causal, high-impact alleles.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale sequencing for rare variants is established for gene discovery, a focused, deep dive into a specific, well-known locus using unprecedented sample sizes to uncover variants with orders-of-magnitude larger effects is a novel strategy to bridge common and rare variant genetics.

proposals.title: Epistatic Interaction Networks of Prioritized Risk Variants in Complex Diseases: A UK Biobank Analysis
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified thousands of loci associated with complex diseases, but they predominantly assume additive genetic effects. This limits biological insight, as non-additive epistatic interactions are a known feature of biological systems and may explain additional disease risk and missing heritability. Testing for epistasis at a genome-wide scale is computationally prohibitive and underpowered, creating a critical need for a targeted, hypothesis-driven approach.
proposals.hypothesis: Prioritized, likely functional variants from established disease-associated loci will exhibit significant, reproducible epistatic interactions that modulate disease risk, and these interactions will be enriched in specific biological pathways.
proposals.method: We will leverage the UK Biobank's genomic and phenotypic data for ~500,000 individuals. First, we will prioritize coding and regulatory variants from known GWAS loci for Crohn's disease and other complex disorders using functional annotations (e.g., CADD scores, chromatin states). We will then systematically test for pairwise statistical interactions between these prioritized variants using a logistic regression framework with an interaction term, adjusting for principal components and main effects. Significant interactions will be validated in independent cohorts (e.g., FinnGen, Biobank Japan) and functionally contextualized using pathway and protein-protein interaction network analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond the additive model to provide a high-confidence map of epistatic networks in complex human diseases. It will identify specific variant pairs that jointly influence risk, offering new mechanistic hypotheses for disease biology. The results will inform more accurate genetic risk prediction models that account for genetic context.
proposals.novelty: Unlike brute-force genome-wide epistasis scans, our approach is focused on biologically prioritized variants from established loci, dramatically increasing statistical power and interpretability. It integrates large-scale biobank data with functional genomics to move from correlation to testable mechanistic models of genetic interaction.

proposals.title: Generative Models for Realistic Synthetic GWAS Summary Statistics to Benchmark Gene Prioritization Methods
proposals.motivation: Current methods for prioritizing disease-associated genes from GWAS summary statistics rely on heuristics and statistical fine-mapping, but their performance is difficult to benchmark due to a lack of realistic, ground-truth data where the causal genes are known. This creates a critical gap in validating tools used to translate genetic discoveries into biological insights for complex diseases. A method to generate realistic synthetic GWAS data with predefined causal gene sets would provide a crucial benchmark for the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model, trained on real large-scale biobank GWAS summary statistics (e.g., from the UK Biobank), can learn the complex correlation structure of the genome and produce realistic synthetic summary statistics that embed a user-specified set of causal genes, thereby enabling rigorous evaluation of gene prioritization algorithms.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) where the generator takes as input a seed vector and a binary mask representing the predefined set of causal genes. The model will be trained on real GWAS summary statistics (e.g., beta and p-value vectors) from the UK Biobank, using linkage disequilibrium (LD) matrices from reference panels (e.g., 1000 Genomes) as a structural prior. The discriminator will be tasked with distinguishing real summary statistic vectors from synthetic ones, conditioned on the LD structure, ensuring the output respects genetic architecture. We will validate synthetic data using metrics of LD score regression and genetic correlation with real traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool, 'SynthGWAS', for generating benchmark datasets with known causal gene sets. It will directly enable the quantitative comparison and validation of gene prioritization and fine-mapping methods, accelerating the translation of GWAS hits into biological mechanisms. The tool will be a resource for method developers and geneticists studying complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: While generative models exist for individual-level genotype data, applying them to the 'dual problem' of generating summary statistics conditioned on a specific gene list is a novel, inverse approach to benchmarking. This moves beyond simple simulation of genotypes to directly modeling the complex statistical outputs used by researchers, a gap not addressed by pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: A Comparative Loss-of-Function Tolerance Atlas for Refining Pathogenic Gene Prioritization in Clinical Exome Sequencing
proposals.motivation: Clinical exome sequencing often yields variants of uncertain significance (VUS) in genes of unknown disease relevance, complicating diagnosis. Pre-2026, large-scale population biobanks like the UK Biobank provide unprecedented data on the frequency of predicted loss-of-function (LoF) variants, which can distinguish highly constrained, disease-associated genes from LoF-tolerant ones. However, existing constraint metrics lack granularity across specific developmental and disease contexts, limiting their utility for prioritizing genes in patients with specific disorder categories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating large-scale LoF tolerance data with gene-specific disease associations from genomic atlases will generate a refined, context-aware prioritization score that significantly outperforms general constraint metrics in identifying pathogenic genes for specific developmental and complex disorders.
proposals.method: We will aggregate predicted LoF variant counts from ~500,000 exomes in the UK Biobank, FinnGen, and All of Us cohorts. Using a Bayesian framework, we will model gene-specific LoF intolerance (pLI) conditioned on disease ontology categories (e.g., developmental disorders, inflammatory diseases) derived from associations in ClinVar, OMIM, and large GWAS. The core algorithm will compute a posterior probability of pathogenicity for a given gene-disorder pair by integrating its global constraint, its specific association strength for the patient's phenotype, and the observed LoF frequency in matched controls from biobank health records.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, ranked gene list and web tool for clinical geneticists, directly improving VUS interpretation. It will provide a quantitative framework linking population genetic constraint to specific disease mechanisms, moving beyond one-size-fits-all gene scores. The results will enhance diagnostic yield in sequencing studies for complex and developmental diseases.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing methods like gnomAD pLI, our approach contextually refines constraint using phenotypic data from biobanks and disease ontologies, creating dynamic rather than static gene scores. This directly addresses the heterogeneity in LoF tolerance across different biological pathways and disease types.

proposals.title: Coalescent-based estimation of loss-of-function allele ages and their association with disease risk in large-scale biobank data
proposals.motivation: Large-scale sequencing studies have cataloged numerous common loss-of-function (LoF) variants, but their evolutionary histories remain poorly characterized. Estimating the ages of these alleles is crucial for understanding their population-specific frequencies, potential roles in adaptation, and differential contributions to disease risk, particularly for complex developmental and immune disorders. Pre-2026 biobanks like the UK Biobank provide the necessary scale and phenotypic depth to link allele age with clinical outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Common LoF variants have a wide distribution of allele ages, and younger, population-specific LoF alleles are more likely to be associated with increased risk for specific complex diseases due to weaker purifying selection and recent changes in selective pressures.
proposals.method: We will apply coalescent theory and site frequency spectrum (SFS) models, specifically using the allele frequency conditional on age (AFoCA) framework and extensions of the `ALDER` and `GEVA` methods, to estimate the age of common LoF variants (MAF > 0.5%) in phased genomic data from the UK Biobank and other large cohorts. We will then perform association testing between estimated allele age and disease status for disorders highlighted in Barrett's recent work (e.g., Crohn's disease), using logistic regression models adjusted for relevant covariates and population structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale map of common LoF allele ages, directly linking evolutionary history with disease risk. It will provide a quantitative framework to distinguish between older, tolerated LoF variants and younger, potentially deleterious ones, refining our understanding of genetic architecture. The results will inform the clinical interpretation of LoF variants in population screening.
proposals.novelty: While coalescent theory has been applied to estimate the age of rare variants and selective sweeps, its systematic application to date common, putatively deleterious LoF variants in a disease-association context is novel. This integrates population genetic inference directly with the analysis of biobank-scale case-control phenotypes.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptional Signatures of Heterozygous Loss-of-Function Variants in Human Peripheral Blood Mononuclear Cells
proposals.motivation: Large-scale genomic studies have identified numerous heterozygous loss-of-function (LoF) variants associated with disease risk, but their functional impact on gene expression in relevant cell types remains poorly understood. Bulk transcriptomics averages signals across millions of cells, masking cell-type-specific and stochastic effects of haploinsufficiency that are critical for understanding incomplete penetrance and variable expressivity in complex disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heterozygous LoF variants in disease-associated genes produce measurable, cell-type-specific transcriptional signatures in single cells, including increased expression variance (transcriptional noise) and subtle, compensatory cis- and trans-regulatory changes not detectable in bulk tissue.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) data from the UK Biobank and other cohorts, focusing on peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from individuals genotyped for rare and common heterozygous LoF variants. Using pre-2026 tools (Seurat for quality control, clustering, and differential expression; Monocle 3 for pseudotime analysis; and SCENIC for regulatory network inference), we will compare expression profiles, variance, and co-expression networks between carrier and non-carrier cells within defined immune cell subtypes. Statistical models will account for technical covariates and population structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the immediate cellular consequences of haploinsufficiency, providing a functional bridge between genetic association studies and disease mechanisms. It will identify which LoF variants cause significant transcriptional dysregulation and define whether specific cell types are more vulnerable, offering insights into cellular pathogenesis and potential biomarkers for disease risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: While population genetics has catalogued LoF variants and bulk transcriptomics has assessed their mean effects, applying single-cell resolution to this question in a large, phenotyped human cohort is novel. This approach uniquely addresses the cell-type specificity and stochasticity of LoF effects, moving beyond aggregate measures of expression.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of human gain-of-function variants through integrative analysis of biobank-scale genomic and phenotypic data
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and large-scale sequencing efforts have predominantly focused on identifying loss-of-function variants and risk alleles associated with disease. However, a systematic framework for identifying gain-of-function (GoF) variants—which confer novel or enhanced molecular activity—is lacking. Characterizing such variants is crucial for understanding adaptive traits, variable drug responses, and rare developmental disorders, yet they remain understudied in human populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating population-scale genomic data with deep phenotypic and biomarker profiles, we can develop and apply a computational pipeline to systematically identify and validate putative GoF variants, particularly those associated with protective or extreme quantitative traits.
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-exome and genome sequencing data from >500,000 individuals in the UK Biobank, FinnGen, and All of Us cohorts. The pipeline will combine: 1) Statistical outlier detection for extreme quantitative phenotypes (e.g., biomarker levels from NMR metabolomics), 2) Association testing for variants with effect directions opposite to known loss-of-function alleles, and 3) Functional prediction using pre-2026 tools like REVEL and CADD, prioritizing missense and regulatory variants in genes intolerant to GoF mutations (using metrics like pLI and LOEUF). Candidate variants will be validated using in silico protein structure modeling (AlphaFold2) and by assessing their co-segregation with phenotypes in familial data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first curated catalog of high-confidence GoF variants in the human population, linking them to protective health metrics, drug metabolism extremes, and rare developmental phenotypes. It will provide a methodological framework for the 'inverse problem' of variant interpretation, moving beyond risk prediction to identify functional enhancements.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies disease-risk mutations, this proposal inverts the paradigm to systematically hunt for functional *enhancements* using biobank data. The novelty lies in the integrative multi-cohort approach combining extreme phenotype analysis, reverse-direction association signals, and GoF-intolerant gene contexts, a strategy not yet deployed at scale.

proposals.title: Global Genetic Architecture of Common Diseases: Validating Polygenic Risk Scores and Prevalence Estimates in Underrepresented Populations
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and polygenic risk score (PRS) models for common diseases are overwhelmingly derived from European-ancestry cohorts, raising concerns about their generalizability. This Eurocentric bias limits the clinical utility of genomic medicine globally and risks exacerbating health disparities. It remains unclear whether the genetic architecture, effect sizes of risk variants, and disease prevalence estimates hold in diverse, understudied populations from Africa, Latin America, and South Asia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genetic architecture of common diseases (e.g., Crohn's disease, type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease) is population-specific, leading to significant miscalibration and reduced predictive accuracy of existing PRS models in non-European populations. Consequently, disease prevalence estimates based on European genetic data will not accurately reflect the burden in global cohorts.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale, cross-population analysis by harmonizing genomic and health data from the UK Biobank with diverse biobanks (e.g., All of Us, Biobank Japan, and cohorts from H3Africa). For target diseases, we will conduct GWAS stratified by genetic ancestry and compute cross-ancestry genetic correlation using LD Score regression. We will train and validate PRS models using PRS-CSx, a Bayesian method for cross-population PRS construction, and assess their predictive accuracy (AUC) and calibration. Prevalence estimates from genetic risk distributions will be compared to clinically recorded disease status across populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of the portability of current genomic medicine tools, identifying diseases and populations where bias is most pronounced. It will deliver improved, population-aware PRS models for understudied groups and produce more accurate global burden estimates, directly informing the design of inclusive genomic studies and equitable clinical translation.
proposals.novelty: While the bias is known, this study systematically quantifies its impact on both risk prediction and prevalence estimation across a wide range of common diseases using a harmonized analytical framework and the largest available aggregation of diverse biobank data pre-2026. The application of advanced cross-population PRS methods like PRS-CSx to this scale of multi-disease, multi-biobank data is novel.

proposals.title: Single-cell transcriptomic profiling reveals cell-type-specific regulatory mechanisms of common disease risk loci in immune cells
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of loci associated with common diseases, but the causal genes, cell types, and regulatory mechanisms remain largely unknown. This gap hinders translation of genetic findings into biological understanding and therapeutic targets. While bulk transcriptomics and biobank-scale genomic analyses have linked variants to broad expression changes, they lack the resolution to pinpoint effects within specific, disease-relevant immune cell populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that common disease-associated risk alleles exert their effects by dysregulating gene expression in specific immune cell types and states, and that single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of patient-derived cells will reveal these cell-type-specific regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will perform scRNA-seq (using 10x Genomics Chromium platform) on peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from 150 individuals, stratified by genotype at key risk loci for Crohn's disease and other immune-mediated disorders identified in large biobanks. Cell types will be annotated using reference-based integration (Seurat v4). Within each cell type, we will perform expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) mapping (using TensorQTL) to identify cell-type-specific cis-eQTLs. We will integrate these results with chromatin accessibility data (public ATAC-seq) to pinpoint candidate causal variants and disrupted regulatory elements.
proposals.contribution: This work will map disease risk variants to specific cell types and molecular pathways, moving beyond genetic association to mechanistic insight. It will generate a publicly available resource of cell-type-specific eQTLs in a patient cohort, directly informing functional follow-up studies. The findings will prioritize cell types and candidate genes for therapeutic intervention in common immune-mediated diseases.
proposals.novelty: This study moves from bulk-tissue and biobank-scale genetic correlation to a focused, mechanistic dissection within primary human immune cells at single-cell resolution. It directly tests the cell-type-specificity of regulatory mechanisms for known risk loci in a genotyped patient cohort, a critical step not addressed by prior large-scale genomic or metabolomic atlas studies.

proposals.title: Genome-wide association and polygenic score analysis of quantitative disease severity and treatment-response sub-phenotypes in inflammatory bowel disease
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified loci for disease susceptibility, but the binary case-control paradigm obscures clinically critical heterogeneity. For complex disorders like Crohn's disease, substantial variation exists in disease course, severity, and treatment response, which are poorly predicted by current genetic risk scores. Moving beyond susceptibility to model this quantitative phenotypic variation is a crucial next step for precision medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic variants, including both common susceptibility loci and rare coding mutations, show specific associations with quantitative measures of disease severity and drug response, and these associations are partially independent of those for disease susceptibility itself.
proposals.method: We will leverage deeply phenotyped cohorts, including the UK Biobank, with genomic data and linked health records. For Crohn's disease, quantitative sub-phenotypes will be derived from electronic health records using natural language processing and procedural codes to create indices for surgical intervention frequency, steroid refractoriness, and hospitalization rates. We will perform GWAS and gene-based burden tests on these quantitative traits, adjusting for case-status polygenic risk. Methods will include linear mixed-model association (BOLT-LMM or SAIGE) for common variants and sequence kernel association tests (SKAT) for rare variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel genetic variants specifically associated with disease progression and treatment outcomes, moving beyond mere susceptibility. It will generate new polygenic scores for predicting severe disease course, which could inform clinical monitoring and trial stratification. The analytical framework will serve as a model for decomposing genetic architecture of other complex developmental and immune-mediated disorders.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Barrett and others has focused on predicting disease case status using metabolomic and genomic data, this proposal inverts that paradigm to genetically dissect the quantitative phenotypic spectrum within cases. It integrates common and rare variant analysis with real-world clinical severity metrics rather than simple diagnostic labels.

proposals.title: Epistatic Interaction Networks Underlying Complex Disease Risk: A Multi-Variant, Multi-Disease Analysis in the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified thousands of independent loci associated with complex diseases, but these signals are typically treated as additive, independent risk factors. This approach fails to capture the epistatic (gene-gene) interactions that are a fundamental component of genetic architecture and are hypothesized to explain a significant portion of the missing heritability for disorders like Crohn's disease. Modeling these interactions is crucial for accurate risk prediction and understanding biological pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant, non-additive epistatic interactions exist between established GWAS loci for Crohn's disease and other immune-mediated disorders, and that modeling these interactions will significantly improve polygenic risk prediction and reveal novel biological networks.
proposals.method: We will leverage the UK Biobank's genomic and biomarker data (NMR atlas) for ~118,461 individuals with deep phenotypic data.
