Starting with established risk loci from large-scale sequencing studies (e.g., for Crohn's disease), we will perform a systematic, genome-wide search for pairwise and higher-order epistasis using a two-stage approach: 1) a fast, screening phase using methods like BOOST or PLINK's fast-epistasis, followed by 2) rigorous, model-based testing (e.g., using linear or logistic regression with interaction terms, adjusting for principal components). Significant interactions will be validated in independent cohorts and functionally annotated using pathway and protein-protein interaction databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond the additive model by providing a high-confidence map of epistatic networks for specific diseases. It will directly test whether epistasis contributes to missing heritability and improves genomic prediction models. The findings could reveal novel therapeutic targets that emerge only in specific genetic contexts.
proposals.novelty: While epistasis screens are common, this proposal is novel in its focus on interactions between *established* GWAS hits rather than a genome-wide blind search, increasing statistical power and biological interpretability. It uniquely integrates dense biomarker phenotypes (the NMR atlas) to assess the functional and intermediate phenotypic consequences of these genetic interactions.

proposals.title: Functional Genomic Characterization of Pleiotropic Loci Underlying Seven Major Complex Diseases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of loci associated with complex diseases, but a significant proportion demonstrate cross-disease associations, suggesting shared biological pathways. Understanding the functional mechanisms of these pleiotropic loci is crucial for deciphering disease etiology, explaining clinical comorbidities, and identifying novel therapeutic targets that may be effective across multiple conditions. This addresses a key gap, as most post-GWAS functional work focuses on disease-specific signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shared genetic risk loci across seven major diseases (e.g., Crohn's disease, coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes) converge on specific cell types and regulatory pathways, and that their functional characterization will reveal core biological mechanisms of systemic dysregulation.
proposals.method: We will integrate summary statistics from large-scale GWAS meta-analyses for seven diseases to identify significant cross-phenotype associations using statistical methods like PLACO (Pleiotropic Analysis under Composite Null). For prioritized pleiotropic loci, we will perform fine-mapping (using SuSiE) to identify credible causal variants. We will then annotate these variants using multi-omic data (e.g., GTEx, ENCODE, Roadmap Epigenomics) to predict target genes and cell-type-specific regulatory effects. Functional validation will involve CRISPR-based perturbation in relevant cell models (e.g., iPSC-derived immune or endothelial cells) followed by transcriptomic (RNA-seq) and chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq) assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map and functionally validate shared genetic architecture across multiple diseases, moving beyond statistical association to mechanistic insight. It will deliver a resource of high-confidence pleiotropic variants, their predicted target genes, and affected pathways, providing a foundational framework for understanding disease co-morbidity and identifying points for broad-spectrum therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While cross-trait GWAS meta-analysis exists, this proposal uniquely combines robust statistical identification of pleiotropic loci with a comprehensive, multi-omic functional genomics pipeline specifically designed to dissect their biological mechanisms, bridging a critical gap between genetic correlation and causal biology.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Robustness and Efficiency of a Shared Control Design for Genome-Wide Association Studies of Co-Occurring Developmental and Metabolic Disorders
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) are resource-intensive, and the use of a single, shared control group across multiple disease phenotypes offers a promising strategy to increase statistical power and cost-efficiency. However, its safety (e.g., risk of false positives due to population stratification or cryptic relatedness) and efficacy (true power gains) have not been systematically validated across diverse, clinically relevant disease sets. This is critical for translating biobank-scale genomic data into actionable insights for complex disease risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A carefully curated, deeply phenotyped shared control group, when coupled with rigorous genomic control and mixed-model correction, will provide statistically robust and materially more efficient association signals for a spectrum of related disorders compared to study-specific controls, without introducing systematic bias.
proposals.method: We will apply the shared control design to UK Biobank data, focusing on a set of co-occurring phenotypes including Crohn's disease, type 2 diabetes, and related metabolic/developmental disorders. The shared control group will be constructed from individuals with no diagnosis in the entire disease set, matched for key covariates. We will use SAIGE (Scalable and Accurate Implementation of Generalized mixed model) for association testing to account for sample relatedness and population stratification. The performance will be benchmarked against traditional, disease-specific GWAS using metrics of genomic inflation, replication in independent cohorts, and novel locus discovery.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, evidence-based framework for the application of shared control designs in large biobanks. It will deliver a validated protocol for increasing GWAS efficiency for disease families and generate novel genetic insights into the shared and distinct etiologies of developmental and metabolic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While shared controls are conceptually appealing, this study provides the first comprehensive, empirical test of their validity and utility for a pre-defined cluster of disorders with epidemiological and potential etiological overlap, moving beyond theoretical efficiency gains to a concrete risk-benefit assessment.

proposals.title: A machine learning framework for integrating polygenic and rare variant signals to predict risk for complex developmental disorders
proposals.motivation: Current polygenic risk scores (PRS) for complex diseases, while valuable, often fail to capture the full spectrum of genetic risk, particularly the contribution of rare variants and non-additive genetic interactions. For many developmental disorders and common diseases, predictive power remains limited, hindering clinical translation. Integrating the 'long tail' of weak polygenic signals with rare, penetrant mutations into a unified model is a critical, unsolved challenge in genomic medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models, trained on large-scale genomic and biobank data, can effectively integrate common variant polygenic risk scores with rare variant burden metrics and gene-set annotations to generate significantly improved, multi-locus risk predictions for specific complex diseases.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing large-scale datasets, such as the UK Biobank, where whole-exome/genome sequencing and dense SNP array data are available for hundreds of thousands of individuals. As input features, we will compute traditional PRS, rare variant burden scores per gene and pathway, and functional annotations. We will train and compare several supervised machine learning algorithms, including gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) and regularized linear models (LASSO), to predict disease status for conditions like Crohn's disease. Model performance will be rigorously evaluated via cross-validation and in independent cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated risk prediction framework that moves beyond additive PRS. It will quantify the marginal predictive gain from incorporating rare variants and epistatic features, providing a more holistic view of individual genetic risk. The resulting models and software will be a resource for researchers aiming to bridge the gap between genomic discovery and clinical risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While PRS and rare variant studies are common, their integration via flexible, non-linear machine learning models for specific disease prediction is not yet standard. This proposal's novelty lies in its systematic, data-driven approach to combining these disparate genetic signals into a single, optimized predictive instrument, moving from a focus on discovery to applied prediction.

proposals.title: A Network Analysis of Shared Genetic Architecture Reveals Functional Modules and Etiological Pathways Across Common Diseases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of loci associated with common diseases, revealing extensive genetic overlap between seemingly distinct disorders. This pleiotropy suggests shared biological pathways, but the systemic organization of these shared genetic effects remains poorly characterized. A network-based framework is needed to move beyond pairwise comparisons and elucidate the higher-order genetic structure connecting disease risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that diseases can be organized into a biologically meaningful network based on shared genetic risk loci, and that this network will reveal distinct clusters (modules) of diseases linked by common etiological pathways.
proposals.method: We will construct a disease-disease network where nodes represent diseases and weighted edges represent the genetic correlation, quantified by LD Score regression, and the significant overlap of associated loci from large-scale GWAS summary statistics (e.g., UK Biobank, FinnGen). We will apply community detection algorithms, specifically the Louvain method for modularity optimization, to identify disease clusters. Network topology metrics (e.g., centrality, clustering coefficient) will be calculated to identify hub diseases and key inter-module connections. Functional enrichment of genes within network modules will be performed using Gene Ontology and pathway databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, systems-level map of the genetic relationships among common human diseases, identifying core etiological modules. It will generate testable hypotheses about shared pathophysiology and highlight potential pleiotropic drug targets. The network will serve as a public resource for understanding comorbidity and refining disease classification.
proposals.novelty: While genetic correlations are commonly reported pairwise, applying formal network theory and community detection to a broad spectrum of diseases to uncover multi-disease functional modules represents a significant advance. This moves beyond cataloging overlap to modeling the emergent structure of pleiotropy.

proposals.title: Ultra-High-Resolution Genome-Wide Association Studies for Developmental Disorders: Uncovering Rare Variants and Regulatory Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Current GWAS arrays, while powerful, capture a limited fraction of human genetic variation, particularly rare and structural variants implicated in disease risk and developmental disorders. As evidenced by large-scale sequencing efforts for Crohn's disease, moving beyond common SNP arrays is critical to explain missing heritability and identify causal genes and pathways. This gap limits the clinical translation of genetic findings into risk prediction and mechanistic understanding for complex diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ultra-high-resolution genotyping arrays, augmented by imputation from large-scale whole-genome sequencing (WGS) reference panels, will identify novel, rare, and regulatory variants associated with developmental and immune-mediated diseases, providing a more complete genetic architecture than standard GWAS.
proposals.method: We will utilize the UK Biobank's ~500,000 participants with extensive phenotypic data. Genotyping will be performed using the Illumina Infinium Global Diversity Array-8 v2.0 or similar, providing > 2 million markers. We will impute to the TOPMed or UK Biobank WGS reference panels (n>100,000) using the Michigan or Sanger imputation servers to achieve ultra-high-resolution variant coverage. Association testing for binary disease traits (e.g., Crohn's, other developmental disorders) and quantitative biomarkers will be conducted using REGENIE for scalable regression, followed by fine-mapping (SuSiE) and colocalization with eQTL data to prioritize causal genes.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a comprehensive atlas of disease-associated genetic variation, including rare variants (MAF 0.1%-1%) and non-coding regulatory elements, for multiple disorders. It will directly translate the scale of biobank resources into improved polygenic risk scores and nominate novel biological mechanisms for experimental validation, bridging genomic discovery and functional genomics.
proposals.novelty: While large biobank GWAS exist, this proposal systematically applies the latest ultra-high-density array technology and the largest available WGS reference panels to a pre-existing deep phenotypic resource, specifically targeting the resolution needed for developmental disorders, moving beyond common variant discovery to a more integrative rare-variant and regulatory variant framework.

proposals.title: Functional Annotation of Non-Coding GWAS Loci for Inflammatory Bowel Disease Using Multi-Omic Profiling in Primary Immune Cells
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of loci associated with complex diseases like Crohn's disease, but the vast majority of associated SNPs lie in non-coding regions, obscuring their functional impact and the genes they regulate. This gap impedes the translation of genetic risk into mechanistic understanding and therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 biobank-scale studies, like the sequencing of 100,000 individuals for Crohn's disease, have powerfully catalogued associations but now require systematic functional follow-up.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq) and gene expression (RNA-seq) profiles from disease-relevant primary immune cell types (e.g., CD4+ T cells, macrophages) with fine-mapped GWAS SNPs will reveal cell-type-specific regulatory circuits, nominate causal genes, and delineate the molecular pathways through which non-coding variants contribute to inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) risk.
proposals.method: We will prioritize fine-mapped, non-coding SNPs from large-scale IBD GWAS and sequencing studies. Using primary immune cells from healthy donors and IBD patients, we will perform ATAC-seq to map open chromatin and RNA-seq to quantify gene expression. We will use computational tools like RASQUAL to perform allele-specific expression and chromatin accessibility QTL mapping, linking risk alleles to molecular phenotypes. Candidate causal variants and genes will be validated using CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) in cell lines followed by phenotypic assays of immune activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond genetic association to provide a functional map of IBD risk loci in physiologically relevant cell types, directly linking non-coding variants to target genes and dysregulated pathways. It will deliver a prioritized list of high-confidence causal genes and regulatory elements for experimental validation and drug discovery, bridging the gap between statistical genetics and disease biology.
proposals.novelty: While QTL studies exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on primary immune cells central to IBD pathology, integrates fine-mapped variants from the latest sequencing studies, and employs a direct, multi-omic QTL framework to solve the 'inverse problem' of moving from genetic signal to function in a disease-specific context.

proposals.title: Genetically-Informed EHR Phenotyping for the Discovery of Clinically Relevant Disease Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Current disease classifications, often based on clinical presentation, are heterogeneous and may obscure distinct biological subtypes with different prognoses and treatment needs. Large-scale biobanks like the UK Biobank now link genetic data to rich, longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs), offering an unprecedented opportunity to refine disease definitions. However, methods to systematically integrate these deep phenotypic trajectories with genetic risk data to uncover etiologically distinct subtypes are underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating polygenic risk scores (PRS) and rare variant burden with longitudinal EHR-derived phenotypic signatures will reveal genetically distinct, clinically actionable subtypes within broad diagnostic categories like inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and metabolic disorders.
proposals.method: We will use the UK Biobank cohort (N~500,000) with linked genomic and EHR data. For target diseases (e.g., Crohn's disease, type 2 diabetes), we will construct deep phenotypic vectors from EHRs using NLP (e.g., cTAKES, CLAMP) and temporal modeling (e.g., RNNs, temporal convolutional networks) to capture sequences of diagnoses, medications, and lab values. We will perform unsupervised clustering (e.g., hierarchical, k-means) on these phenotypic vectors and test for enrichment of specific genetic architectures (common variant PRS, rare variant burden in known and novel genes from exome sequencing) across clusters. Significant genetic associations will validate biologically distinct subtypes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for genetically-anchored disease subtyping, moving beyond correlation to causal inference. It will deliver specific, genetically-defined subtypes with distinct clinical trajectories, potentially identifying groups for targeted screening or repurposed therapies. The methods will be generalizable to other complex diseases, enhancing precision medicine.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links genetics to single disease codes or biomarkers, this proposal uniquely uses deep, temporal EHR phenotyping as the substrate for clustering, then seeks genetic validation, reversing the standard GWAS paradigm to discover subtypes rather than risk loci for pre-defined entities.

proposals.title: Constrained Integrative Haplotype History (CIHH): A Model-Checking Framework for Resolving Non-Identifiability in Disease-Associated Haplotype Evolution
proposals.motivation: Inferring the evolutionary history of disease-associated haplotypes is crucial for understanding the roles of selection and demography in shaping genetic risk. However, this is a fundamentally non-identifiable problem: many distinct demographic and selective histories can produce identical patterns of modern haplotype diversity. This ambiguity undermines robust inference about the evolutionary forces acting on risk loci identified in large-scale genomic studies of common diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating direct temporal anchors from ancient DNA with forward genomic simulations and a rigorous model-checking procedure, we can reject statistically plausible but non-predictive evolutionary narratives, yielding robust, testable inferences about haplotype history.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first uses time-structured ancient DNA data to constrain the parameter space for demographic and selective models. We will then generate an ensemble of plausible evolutionary trajectories for target haplotypes using forward population genomic simulators (e.g., SLiM). The core innovation is a model-checking and selection procedure that validates these trajectories against orthogonal data not used in model fitting, such as their ability to predict the spatial distribution of the haplotype in independent, geographically distinct populations or the statistical fine-mapping signals from independent GWAS summary statistics for the same trait.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable framework for moving beyond mere statistical plausibility to predictive, validated models of haplotype evolution. It will deliver more robust interpretations of how selection and demography have shaped specific risk haplotypes for common diseases, directly informing the evolutionary context of findings from large biobanks and sequencing studies.
proposals.novelty: While ancient DNA has been used for demographic inference and forward simulations for testing selection, their tight integration within a formal model-checking paradigm using orthogonal GWAS and population genetic data as validation targets is a novel approach to resolving haplotype history non-identifiability.

proposals.title: Mapping Regulatory Fault Lines: Integrating Haplotype Structure with 3D Genomic Architecture to Prioritize Functional Risk Variants for Complex Diseases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of disease-associated loci, but the majority reside in non-coding regions, complicating the identification of causal variants and their target genes. Existing methods for functional interpretation often treat linkage disequilibrium (LD) blocks and regulatory annotations separately, lacking a framework to systematically predict which specific regulatory elements within a risk haplotype are most vulnerable to disruption. This gap hinders the transition from genetic association to mechanistic understanding of disease etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution haplotype maps with three-dimensional functional genomic data (e.g., chromatin contacts, enhancer-promoter loops) will reveal specific regulatory connections that are disproportionately enriched for haplotypic variation, thereby pinpointing the non-coding elements and gene targets most likely to mediate disease risk.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that treats phased haplotype data from biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) as a 'hazard map' of genetic variation. This will be spatially overlaid onto population-averaged 3D genomic architectures derived from Hi-C and promoter capture Hi-C data in relevant cell types. Using a graph-based model where nodes are regulatory elements and edges are chromatin contacts, we will simulate the propagation of haplotype-specific variant burden through the network. We will prioritize 'regulatory fault lines'—high-contact genomic segments where a high density of candidate regulatory variants coincides—for experimental validation using massively parallel reporter assays (MPRA) in disease-relevant cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrative framework for moving beyond statistical association to generate testable, mechanistic hypotheses about non-coding risk variants. It will deliver a prioritized set of regulatory elements and gene targets for complex diseases, directly informing functional follow-up studies. The method will be a resource for the community, enabling more efficient translation of GWAS findings.
proposals.novelty: While haplotype and 3D genomic analyses exist in parallel, the explicit conceptual and computational integration of LD structure as a hazard layer onto physical chromatin interaction networks is novel. This approach moves beyond colocalization to actively model the structural vulnerability of the regulatory landscape to genetic variation.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Constraint and Plasticity in Complex Trait Architecture: A Cross-Population and Cross-Species LD-Based Analysis
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of loci for complex diseases, but their interpretation is hindered by linkage disequilibrium (LD). Most findings are from European-ancestry cohorts, limiting understanding of generalizability and biological mechanism. Comparing LD patterns and fine-mapped variants across diverse human ancestries and with model organisms provides an evolutionary framework to distinguish fundamental biological constraints from lineage-specific adaptations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic architectures for core developmental and metabolic traits will show greater evolutionary constraint (conserved LD patterns and orthologous variant associations across species and human populations) than architectures for immune or neuropsychiatric traits, which will exhibit greater evolutionary plasticity and population-specific signatures.
proposals.method: We will apply LD-aware fine-mapping (e.g., SuSiE) to orthologous genomic regions for 50+ complex traits, using summary statistics from large biobanks (UK Biobank, All of Us) representing diverse ancestries. We will compute and compare LD matrices across populations (e.g., 1000 Genomes super-populations) at these loci. Parallel analysis will be conducted in model organisms (mouse, zebrafish) using QTL and phenomic data to assess conservation of variant-trait associations. A Bayesian hierarchical model will classify loci into an evolutionary hierarchy based on cross-species and cross-population concordance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a ranked atlas of complex trait loci by their degree of evolutionary constraint, directly informing the generalizability of GWAS findings across ancestries. It will identify high-constraint loci as prime candidates for functional validation and highlight plastic loci where environment or local adaptation modulates genetic risk, refining disease etiological models.
proposals.novelty: While cross-population GWAS and cross-species comparisons exist separately, this is the first integrated framework using LD as an evolutionary metric to systematically classify trait architecture. It moves beyond cataloging associations to interpreting their evolutionary history and biological stability.

proposals.title: Rare Variant Burden in IRGM and Associated Loci Explains Missing Heritability in Common Variant-Negative Crohn's Disease Cases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified numerous common variants associated with Crohn's disease (CD), but a significant proportion of heritability remains unexplained. Furthermore, a subset of patients lacks a strong burden of these common risk alleles, suggesting alternative genetic architectures, such as rare, penetrant variants, may drive disease in these 'common variant-negative' cases. Resolving this missing heritability is critical for understanding disease etiology and improving genetic risk prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rare coding and regulatory variants in the IRGM locus and other GWAS-implicated genes contribute significantly to disease risk in CD patients who do not carry a high burden of common risk variants identified by polygenic risk scores (PRS).
proposals.method: We will analyze whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing data from large cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank, IBD sequencing consortia). Cases will be stratified by a common-variant PRS into PRS-negative and PRS-positive groups. Using gene-based burden tests (e.g., SKAT-O, Firth's regression) and optimized sequence kernel association tests, we will compare the aggregate burden of rare (MAF<0.01), predicted deleterious variants in IRGM and other candidate loci between PRS-negative cases and controls. This will be complemented by colocalization analysis of rare variant signals with GWAS and eQTL signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the contribution of rare variants to CD heritability in a specific, unresolved patient subgroup. It will identify specific genes and variant classes that confer risk independent of common variants, refining genetic models of CD. The findings could inform more targeted sequencing strategies for diagnosis and risk assessment in families lacking common risk alleles.
proposals.novelty: While rare variants in genes like NOD2 are known CD risk factors, a focused investigation of their role specifically in common variant-negative cases, using PRS-based stratification and advanced burden testing on large-scale sequencing data, represents a novel and targeted approach to resolving missing heritability.

proposals.title: Integrating eQTL and sQTL Maps in Immune Cell Subtypes to Decipher the Functional Impact of Common Disease-Associated SNPs
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of SNPs associated with immune-mediated and developmental disorders, but the majority reside in non-coding regions, obscuring their functional mechanisms. Pre-2026 work, including large-scale biobank studies, highlights the need to link these risk variants to specific molecular phenotypes, such as gene expression and splicing, to understand disease etiology. Immune cells are highly relevant for many common diseases, yet cell-type-specific regulatory effects of SNPs remain incompletely characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of common SNPs associated with immune and developmental disorders exert their risk effects by modulating gene expression (eQTLs) or alternative splicing (sQTLs) in specific immune cell subtypes, and that integrating these maps will reveal novel, cell-type-specific regulatory pathways.
proposals.method: We will perform cis-eQTL and cis-sQTL mapping using RNA-seq data from purified primary immune cell subsets (e.g., CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, B cells, monocytes) from ~500 healthy donors. Association testing between genotype dosages and normalized gene expression/transcript ratios will be conducted using linear regression models in TensorQTL, covarying for technical and genetic factors. Significant QTLs will be colocalized with GWAS loci for Crohn's disease and other immune disorders using statistical methods like COLOC to prioritize causal genes and cell types.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a high-resolution atlas of regulatory QTLs across human immune cell types, directly linking non-coding GWAS variants to their molecular consequences. It will identify the specific immune cell contexts in which disease-associated variants are active and nominate effector genes for functional validation, moving beyond genetic association to mechanistic insight.
proposals.novelty: While bulk tissue eQTL maps exist, this proposal focuses on a systematic, parallel eQTL/sQTL analysis in multiple primary immune cell subtypes, enabling the discovery of cell-type-specific splicing regulation as a key mechanism for common disease SNPs, which has been less explored than expression effects.

proposals.title: A Multi-Gene Humanized Mouse Model of Crohn's Disease Reveals Synergistic Effects of IRGM, NKX2-3, and PTPN2 Risk Alleles on Intestinal Homeostasis and Immune Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified over 200 risk loci for Crohn's disease (CD), yet the functional consequences of most alleles remain poorly understood. A major challenge is that disease risk is polygenic, but most existing mouse models focus on single genes, failing to capture the complex genetic architecture revealed by large-scale biobank studies. Investigating how combinations of non-coding and coding variants in key loci like IRGM (autophagy), NKX2-3 (intestinal development), and PTPN2 (immune signaling) interact to disrupt gut homeostasis is critical for understanding CD etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining human-relevant risk alleles from IRGM, NKX2-3, and PTPN2 in a single mouse model will produce a synergistic, polygenic phenotype that more accurately recapitulates the penetrance and pathophysiological features of human Crohn's disease than single-gene models.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing in C57BL/6J mice to introduce human risk-associated non-coding single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) at the orthologous mouse Irgm and Nkx2-3 loci, based on fine-mapping data from the UK Biobank and International IBD Genetics Consortium. For Ptpn2, we will introduce a loss-of-function mutation analogous to a human CD-associated rare variant. These alleles will be combined through breeding to generate mice carrying all three risk variants. Phenotyping will include longitudinal assessment of intestinal permeability, histopathology, and immune cell profiling by flow cytometry. Response to dextran sodium sulfate (DSS) challenge will be quantified. Multi-omic analysis (RNA-seq and metabolomic profiling of intestinal tissue) will identify dysregulated pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, genetically precise polygenic mouse model for Crohn's disease, providing a critical experimental system to dissect gene-gene interactions and their downstream molecular consequences. It will directly test the polygenic threshold model of CD and identify convergent biological pathways disrupted by multiple risk alleles, offering new targets for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.novelty: While single-gene models for these loci exist, no pre-2026 model combines human-specific GWAS-derived non-coding variants from multiple loci to study their additive and epistatic effects in vivo, moving beyond monogenic to truly polygenic disease modeling.

proposals.title: Direct Functional Variant Assay (DFVA): A Framework for Prioritizing Causal Variants in Genome-Wide Association Studies of Complex Diseases
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully identified thousands of tag SNPs associated with disease risk, but these markers are often in linkage disequilibrium with many variants, obscuring the true causal mechanisms. This indirect approach complicates the translation of genetic associations into biological insights and therapeutic targets. A paradigm shift towards directly assaying functional variants is needed to bridge the gap between statistical association and causal biology in complex disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: Prioritizing the direct, multiplexed assay of putatively functional variants (e.g., coding, regulatory, splice-altering) in large-scale cohorts will identify causal genetic effects with greater biological resolution and higher per-variant effect sizes than traditional tag SNP-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a bioinformatic pipeline to curate a high-priority variant set from existing whole-genome sequencing data (e.g., UK Biobank, gnomAD), integrating functional annotations from ENCODE, GTEx, and ClinVar to select variants predicted to alter protein function or gene regulation. Using this panel, we will design a targeted sequencing array or a high-throughput, multiplexed genotyping assay (e.g., based on Illumina's Infinium or a custom amplicon-seq protocol) to directly genotype these variants in large disease cohorts. Association testing will be performed using established statistical genetics models (e.g., REGENIE, SAIGE) to compare the power and biological interpretability of results against imputed GWAS data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework and proof-of-concept for moving beyond tag SNPs, directly implicating specific genetic alterations in disease etiology. It will deliver a curated, functionally annotated variant panel for complex diseases and demonstrate improved fine-mapping resolution, accelerating the identification of candidate causal genes and variants for functional validation.
proposals.novelty: While fine-mapping is standard, this proposal inverts the standard GWAS workflow by designing the initial genotyping platform explicitly for functional variants, rather than using tag SNPs as proxies. It systematically operationalizes the direct assay of causal variants as a primary study design, not just a secondary analysis.

proposals.title: Modeling Recombination Hotspot Architecture to Resolve Untaggable Common Variants in Complex Disease Risk Loci
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have successfully mapped thousands of common disease-associated loci, but a significant limitation is the 'untaggable' SNP problem: many common variants in high-linkage disequilibrium (LD) regions cannot be statistically distinguished from the lead GWAS signal, hindering causal variant identification. This is directly influenced by local recombination rate heterogeneity, yet current recombination hotspot models are coarse and insufficient for fine-mapping. Refining these models is critical for progressing from association to mechanism in post-GWAS functional genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution recombination maps with local genomic features (e.g., PRDM9 motifs, chromatin state, GC content) will generate predictive models of fine-scale recombination that can statistically resolve untaggable common variants, thereby improving the prioritization of likely causal SNPs in complex disease loci.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale haplotype data from biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank) and consortiums (e.g., 1000 Genomes) to construct population-specific, high-resolution recombination maps using established LD-based inference methods (e.g., LDhat). We will train gradient boosting machine (GBM) models, such as XGBoost, to predict recombination rates from a curated set of genomic annotations. These models will be used to generate probabilistic estimates of recombination breakpoints within GWAS loci, which will be integrated into a conditional testing framework (e.g., using FINEMAP or SuSiE) to re-evaluate the association evidence for untaggable SNPs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available resource of fine-scale recombination probability maps and a statistical framework for integrating recombination uncertainty into fine-mapping. It will directly address a key bottleneck in translating GWAS findings by providing a principled method to prioritize candidate causal variants in high-LD regions, accelerating downstream functional validation.
proposals.novelty: While recombination hotspots are known, their predictive modeling for the specific purpose of resolving untaggable SNPs in disease loci is novel. This approach moves beyond using recombination as a static covariate to dynamically informing fine-mapping probability models.

proposals.title: Linking Non-Coding Risk Variants to Causal Genes in Complex Diseases via Population-Scale Hi-C Interaction Mapping
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of non-coding risk SNPs for complex diseases, but their biological interpretation is hindered by the difficulty of linking them to their true target genes. The majority of these variants lie in regulatory regions, and their effects are likely mediated through three-dimensional chromatin architecture. Pre-2026, large-scale Hi-C data resources like those from the 4D Nucleome Consortium and population-specific maps are emerging, but their systematic integration with GWAS findings for disease gene discovery remains incomplete.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution Hi-C chromatin interaction maps with large-scale GWAS summary statistics will accurately link non-coding risk SNPs to their true target genes, revealing novel disease-associated pathways and improving polygenic risk prediction for specific disorders.
proposals.method: We will utilize high-resolution Hi-C data from relevant cell types and tissues (e.g., immune cells, intestinal epithelium) from public repositories and generate new data for key disease-relevant states. We will apply established computational pipelines, such as Fit-Hi-C and HiC-Pro, to identify statistically significant chromatin loops. These interaction maps will be integrated with pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics for Crohn's disease and other immune-mediated disorders from large biobanks (e.g., UK Biobank). We will use a Bayesian colocalization framework (e.g., COLOC) and Mendelian Randomization to prioritize gene targets where a risk SNP's interaction signal and a gene's expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) colocalize within the same chromatin loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated atlas of SNP-to-gene links for major complex diseases, moving beyond simple genomic proximity. It will nominate novel causal genes and regulatory mechanisms, providing testable hypotheses for functional validation. The refined gene sets will enhance the biological interpretability of polygenic risk scores and inform the prioritization of therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While Hi-C has been used to study chromatin architecture, the novelty lies in the systematic, population-scale integration of tissue-specific Hi-C maps with the massive GWAS datasets now available (e.g., >100,000 individuals for Crohn's disease), applying rigorous statistical colocalization to move from correlation to causal inference for non-coding variants.

proposals.title: Cell-type-specific genetic architecture of inflammatory bowel disease risk using single-cell eQTL mapping in the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for diseases like Crohn's disease have identified numerous risk loci, but their functional interpretation remains a major challenge. Most genetic effects are assumed to act in specific cell types, yet bulk-tissue analyses obscure these critical cellular contexts. Resolving this ambiguity is essential for understanding disease mechanisms and identifying therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of genetic variants associated with Crohn's disease and related immune-mediated disorders exert their effects by modulating gene expression in specific immune (e.g., CD4+ T cells, macrophages) or gut epithelial cell types, and that mapping these effects will reveal novel, context-dependent biological pathways.
proposals.method: We will integrate UK Biobank genetic data with newly generated single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) profiles from peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) and, where available, gut biopsy samples from a subset of participants. Using a pseudobulk approach, we will perform cell-type-specific expression quantitative trait locus (eQTL) mapping for major immune and epithelial lineages. We will then apply statistical colocalization (e.g., COLOC) and Mendelian randomization frameworks to link cell-type-specific eQTLs with disease-associated variants from large-scale GWAS, including the latest IBD meta-analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale atlas of cell-type-specific genetic effects for immune-mediated diseases within the UK Biobank, directly linking risk variants to their cellular targets. It will prioritize causal genes and cell types for functional follow-up, moving beyond association to mechanism. The findings will refine polygenic risk prediction by incorporating cell-context information.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell eQTL studies existed pre-2026, their application at scale within a deeply phenotyped, disease-enriched cohort like the UK Biobank to dissect the genetic architecture of specific complex diseases is novel. The integration of colocalization with cell-type-resolution maps provides a direct path from variant to function in a precise cellular context.

In studies of complex brain disorders like schizophrenia, researchers successfully combine eQTL mapping with computational cell-type deconvolution of bulk tissue RNA-seq data to pinpoint disease-associated regulatory variants acting in specific neural cell types. We adapt this solution by applying the same deconvolution approaches (e.g., CIBERSORTx, MuSiC) to existing bulk tissue eQTL datasets from T2D-relevant tissues—pancreatic islets, adipose tissue, and liver. This will enable mapping of T2D GWAS risk variants to gene expression changes in specific cell populations (e.g., pancreatic beta cells, adipocytes, hepatocytes), moving beyond whole-tissue analysis to reveal the precise cellular contexts in which genetic risk variants exert their regulatory effects on diabetes susceptibility.

It aims to determine whether specific, common haplotypes containing risk variants alter gene expression or regulatory activity in response to environmental factors like diet or metabolic state. By overlaying interaction study results with haplotype-resolved functional annotations (e.g., eQTLs, chromatin accessibility), we can pinpoint which haplotype backgrounds confer environmental sensitivity or resilience at the molecular level. This creates a mechanistic bridge between epidemiological interaction findings and the causal regulatory circuits that drive them.

ideas.title: Deep Seismic Imaging of Genetic Loci: Uncovering Rare Structural Variants as Major Effect Drivers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that common SNPs explain most heritability by seeking rare, high-impact variants within GWAS loci, analogous to how geophysics moved beyond surface models to find deep crustal structures. In geophysics, deep seismic reflection profiling revealed hidden tectonic features with major effects. Adapted here, the solution is to use advanced, deep-coverage long-read sequencing as a 'genomic seismic profiler' within associated loci. This aims to systematically discover rare structural and complex variants, testing if these previously unresolved features, rather than the common SNP tags, are the primary functional drivers of phenotypic variation and missing heritability.

ideas.title: Comparative Evolutionary Genomics of Complex Trait Architecture Across Diverse Human Ancestries
ideas.description: This research direction applies the LD analysis framework to orthologous genomic regions associated with complex traits, conducting parallel investigations in diverse human ancestral populations and multiple model organisms. By comparing how LD patterns and fine-mapped causal variants differ across human populations and are conserved or divergent in other species, it becomes possible to identify which genetic architectures are fundamental to mammalian biology versus those shaped by recent human evolutionary history or local adaptation. This comparative, cross-species approach provides a powerful evolutionary lens to interpret population-specific findings, distinguishing shared biological mechanisms from demographic or selective events unique to specific human lineages. The ultimate goal is to build an evolutionary hierarchy of genetic constraint and plasticity for complex trait loci, informing both biological mechanism and the generalizability of genetic associations.

ideas.title: Employ Instrumental Variables from Econometrics for Causal Inference in T2D
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established instrumental variable (IV) framework from econometrics to enhance Mendelian randomization (MR) studies in Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) research. In econometrics, IVs are used to infer causality from observational data by leveraging a variable that affects the outcome only through its effect on the exposure. The analogous solution involves a strict methodology for validating the instrument's suitability based on core assumptions. Adapted to T2D, this means applying a more rigorous, econometric-style validation to genetic variants used as instruments in MR. The research would focus on formally testing the genetic instrument's relevance (association with the risk factor), independence (no confounding), and exclusion restriction (no direct effect on T2D outside the risk factor pathway). This adaptation promotes standardized sensitivity analyses to detect and correct for pleiotropy, and encourages the design of MR studies that mimic econometric 'natural experiments' using polygenic risk scores or population-specific genetic variations to more credibly establish causal relationships between lifestyle, metabolic factors, and T2D development.

ideas.title: Establish a Common Coordinate Framework for Metabolic Trait Genetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroscience, where integrating disparate brain data was achieved through common coordinate frameworks like reference atlases. The analogous problem in metabolic genetics is the siloed analysis of related traits (T2D, obesity, insulin resistance) across different consortia. The adapted solution is to develop a 'Metabolic Trait Genetic Atlas'—a standardized computational and biological reference framework. This framework would allow genetic findings from various consortia studies to be mapped onto a unified coordinate system defined by genomic, cellular, and pathway axes. This enables true integration, moving beyond comparison to allow for systematic, atlas-based queries to discover shared genetic regulatory modules, distinguish trait-specific signals, and elucidate causal pathways across the spectrum of metabolic disease.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Pipeline for Pre-screening and Prospectively Testing Gene-Environment Interactions
ideas.description: This research employs a two-stage causal framework to overcome the high false-discovery rate of agnostic interaction searches. First, a hypothesis-generating stage uses Mendelian Randomization in biobank data to identify putative causal interactions between genetic risk scores and modifiable environmental exposures. Second, a pre-registered, prospective cohort study rigorously tests these prioritized interactions, modeling them on the additive scale and incorporating objective biomarkers of exposure and intermediate phenotypes to validate mechanistic pathways and ensure replicable findings.

ideas.title: Integrate haplotype analysis with functional genomic data
ideas.description: This idea proposes to combine LD and haplotype maps with gene expression and regulatory element annotations for functional interpretation. The analogy is drawn from structural engineering and seismology, where geological fault maps are integrated with building structural models to predict failure points under stress. The adapted solution treats genetic linkage maps as hazard maps and overlays them onto 3D functional genomic architectures (e.g., from Hi-C and epigenomic data) to simulate and identify the specific regulatory elements and genes most vulnerable to disruption by haplotypic variation, thereby prioritizing candidates for mechanistic study.

ideas.title: A Constrained Integrative Framework to Resolve Non-Identifiable Haplotype History
ideas.description: This research develops a model-testing framework that overcomes the fundamental identifiability problem in inferring haplotype history. It integrates time-structured ancient DNA data as direct temporal anchors with forward population genomic simulations to define plausible evolutionary trajectories. The core advance is a rigorous model-checking and selection procedure that uses orthogonal validation, such as predicting independent GWAS signals or patterns in geographically distinct populations, to reject narratives that are merely statistically plausible but not predictive. This yields robust, testable inferences about the relative roles of selection and demography in shaping haplotype dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Unmixing to Deconvolve T2D Genetic Architecture
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) cases represent the same etiology by adapting a solution from geophysics. In seismology, blind source separation techniques like Independent Component Analysis are used to deconvolve a single, mixed seismic signal into its independent source components (e.g., different earthquake events). Analogously, we propose applying these computational unmixing methods to the 'bulk' genetic and multi-omic data from a T2D cohort. The adapted research idea is to treat the aggregated disease-associated signals as a mixture of independent biological processes. By computationally deconvolving the data into distinct, statistically independent components—each hypothesized to represent a specific etiological pathway (e.g., primary beta-cell dysfunction, severe insulin resistance, lipid-induced metabolic dysfunction)—we can identify heterogeneous disease mechanisms masked in standard bulk analyses and subsequently map individual patients to these refined genetic subtypes.

ideas.title: Calibrating Genetic Maps Across Populations Using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of ecological niche modeling (ENM) from biogeography to the problem of assessing the generalizability of genetic loci across populations. In ENM, a species' environmental tolerances, modeled from its native range, are projected onto new geographic areas to predict suitability; areas where predictions fail indicate limits to the model's transferability. Analogously, we propose treating a genetic association discovered in one population (e.g., Europeans) as a model defined by its local genomic context (LD structure, allele frequency, haplotype background). This model is then systematically 'projected' via fine-mapping into diverse ancestral populations. The goal is not merely to replicate the signal, but to rigorously test the boundaries of its transferability: to identify where the model holds, where it breaks down, and the specific genomic or demographic factors (e.g., divergent LD, allele absence, different causal variants) responsible. This provides a principled framework for moving from binary replication assessments to a nuanced understanding of the portability of genetic findings.

ideas.title: Causal Network Elucidation for T2D via Integrated Computational Inference and Systematic Perturbation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach first uses advanced bioinformatics (e.g., context-aware network reconstruction from multi-omics cohorts) to identify high-confidence modules where T2D-associated genes cluster. The key innovation is the direct, iterative integration of experimental causal screening: top-predicted driver genes and network edges are functionally tested using CRISPR perturbations in relevant cell models. This cycle of computational prediction and empirical validation filters out spurious associations, confirming which network disruptions directly alter diabetic phenotypes. The final output is a causally-annotated, tissue-specific network model of T2D progression, highlighting high-value therapeutic targets.

ideas.title: Mechanistically-Validated Causal Subtyping of T2D via Multi-Omic Integration and Experimental Interrogation
ideas.description: This project moves beyond genetic clustering to define T2D subtypes with validated causal mechanisms. It integrates genetic data with deep, longitudinal phenotypic and multi-omic profiles (e.g., metabolomics, proteomics) to identify subgroups, then applies causal inference methods to prioritize driver pathways. Each putative subtype is then validated by testing its specific molecular hypotheses through targeted experimental perturbations in relevant cellular models, ensuring the subtypes represent true etiological heterogeneity rather than statistical artifacts.

ideas.title: A Multi-Tissue Communication Network Approach to T2D Pathogenesis
ideas.description: This project moves beyond cataloging genes in individual tissues to define the causal communication networks between liver, muscle, and adipose tissue that drive systemic insulin resistance and T2D. It integrates tissue-specific genetic manipulations in a diabetic model organism with concurrent multi-omics profiling (transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics) of all three tissues and plasma. Causal network inference and metabolic flux analysis are then used to identify key hub genes and signaling molecules that mediate pathogenic cross-talk. The functional necessity of these hubs for system-wide metabolic dysfunction is then validated through targeted disruption of the identified communication axes.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising for Rare Haplotype Visualization
ideas.description: This idea expands rare variant haplotype analysis by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, denoising techniques like array processing and wavelet-based filtering are used to extract weak, coherent seismic signals from overwhelming background noise. We adapt this solution to genetics by conceptualizing common variant linkage disequilibrium (LD) as the background noise and rare haplotype associations as the weak target signals. The proposed research would develop and implement novel bioinformatics algorithms that apply multi-locus wavelet decomposition or matched filtering against common haplotype templates. This will computationally isolate the LD signatures of rare haplotypes, enabling their clear visualization and statistical assessment in genetic association studies, thereby enhancing the handling and interpretation of low-frequency variant data.

ideas.title: Relax assumption of tissue-independent eQTL effects
ideas.description: Prioritize variants using context-specific regulatory maps rather than bulk tissue eQTLs.

ideas.title: Direct Inference from Mixed Signals via Blind Source Separation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that phased haplotype input is required for many genetic analyses. Drawing an analogy from signal processing, where blind source separation techniques recover original signals from mixtures without knowing the mixing parameters, we propose to adapt this framework to genetics. The analogous solution uses methods like Independent Component Analysis to separate sources based on their statistical independence. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to develop novel statistical models that treat the pair of haplotypes as latent, independent 'sources' that are mixed in the observed unphased genotypes. By optimizing models to find latent haplotype effects that are statistically independent or exhibit sparse associations, we can perform tasks like association testing or estimating haplotype-specific effects directly from genotype data, eliminating the errors and biases introduced by standard statistical phasing pipelines.

ideas.title: Joint, Identifiable Inference of Demography from Multi-Signal Genomic Data
ideas.description: This project develops a unified inference framework that combines linkage disequilibrium maps with complementary genomic summaries like the site frequency spectrum and ancestral tract lengths to overcome the non-identifiability of demography from LD alone. The core method uses extensive simulations to build an empirical model of the joint distribution of all signals under a wide range of demographic histories, enabling explicit tests for parameter identifiability. By leveraging multiple, partially independent signals, the approach constrains the solution space to deliver statistically robust and interpretable estimates of population size changes, splits, and admixture events. The output includes not only point estimates but also quantified uncertainties and diagnostics highlighting regions of parameter space where inference remains ambiguous.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Uncertainty-Integrated Analysis of Unphased Genotypes
ideas.description: This method performs genetic association and related analyses directly on unphased genotype data by dynamically modeling phase uncertainty within the specific analytical context. It employs a focused stochastic sampling or likelihood-weighted summation over plausible haplotype configurations at loci relevant to the statistical model, guided by local LD patterns and optional pedigree information. This approach avoids the errors of ignoring phase and the computational infeasibility of exhaustive enumeration, providing robust, tractable inference. The result is a unified framework that delivers the accuracy of phased-data methods without requiring a separate, error-prone phasing pre-processing step.

ideas.title: A Confounder-Aware Framework for Comparative Linkage Disequilibrium Analysis Across Species
ideas.description: This project develops a robust analytical system for comparative LD studies that directly addresses the major confounders preventing valid cross-species comparisons. The core innovation is a pre-processing and normalization module that quantifies and corrects for differences in genome assembly completeness, variant calling bias, population sample size, and demographic history before applying the LD framework. The system outputs both corrected, comparable LD metrics and a 'confounder report' quantifying the adjustments made for each species, enabling rigorous evolutionary inferences about recombination, selection, and population history across the tree of life.

ideas.title: Frequency-Normalized Informational Linkage Disequilibrium (FNILD)
ideas.description: This approach quantifies linkage disequilibrium using a normalized information-theoretic metric that is robust to allele frequency differences. It calculates the observed mutual information between loci and compares it to the theoretical maximum mutual information possible given their marginal allele frequency distributions. The core output is a normalized FNILD statistic ranging from 0 (linkage equilibrium) to 1 (complete non-random association), analogous to D' but grounded in information theory. This allows for direct, interpretable comparison of LD strength across diverse genomic regions and population samples, overcoming the primary interpretability flaw of raw mutual information.

ideas.title: Information-Theoretic Network Inference for Functional LD Blocks
ideas.description: This research direction fuses information theory with graph-based visualization to move beyond symmetric correlation measures of LD. By using metrics like mutual information and transfer entropy, we can quantify non-linear dependencies and even temporal or causal directions between loci (e.g., from regulatory variant to target). These asymmetric, information-theoretic weights are then used to construct and visualize a directed network graph. The synergy enables the force-directed layout to visually separate and cluster SNPs into functional modules—such as putative regulatory hubs, haplotype blocks under selection, or causal pathways—providing an intuitive map of the genetic architecture's functional constraints that standard LD metrics and undirected graphs cannot reveal.

ideas.title: Automate tag SNP selection for association studies

ideas.title: Adapting Comparative Linguistics Methods for Multi-Ethnic Haplotype Analysis
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from comparative linguistics. In linguistics, scholars compare diverse languages by identifying cognates (shared due to common descent) and borrowings (shared due to contact), mapping their relationships through phylogenetic trees and networks. The analogous solution involves systematic comparison accounting for historical divergence and horizontal exchange. Adapted to genetics, the new research idea is to develop methods within Haploview to compare haplotype structure across multi-ethnic cohorts not just descriptively, but phylogenetically. The software would be extended to algorithmically distinguish haplotype blocks that are ancestrally shared (analogous to cognates) from those that signal recent gene flow or convergent selection (analogous to borrowings), creating a unified visualization and analysis framework for the evolutionary relationships among haplotypes across diverse human populations.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Reliability Theory to Model Genetic-Environmental Interaction Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Systems Engineering and Network Reliability to investigate gene-gene and gene-environment interactions. In that field, the reliability of complex systems with interacting components (like power grids) is analyzed using probabilistic network models, fault trees, and simulation to predict system-wide failure from individual component failures and their dependencies. The adapted idea is to model genetic risk variants and environmental factors (e.g., dietary markers, obesity metrics) as nodes in an interaction network, where disease onset is the system failure. Using large-scale phenotypic and genomic data, researchers can apply network reliability algorithms to estimate the conditional probability of disease for specific multi-node states, thereby quantifying how synergistic or antagonistic interactions within the genetic-environmental network modulate overall disease risk beyond simple additive effects.

ideas.title: A Pleiotropy-Robust, Multivariable Mendelian Randomization Framework for Causal Inference in T2D
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond standard two-sample MR by building a system inherently resilient to horizontal pleiotropy, the primary threat to MR validity. It employs multivariable MR to simultaneously model multiple correlated risk factors, isolating their independent effects, and runs a battery of sensitivity methods to detect and correct for invalid instruments. Finally, it requires triangulation of evidence from colocalization and biologically grounded cis-variant analyses, ensuring that any inferred causal relationship between a risk factor and T2D is not driven by hidden pleiotropic pathways.

ideas.title: Model Genetic Effects as Composite Signals Using Non-Linear Filtering
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from signal processing, where complex signals are decomposed using non-linear filters to isolate specific components. The analogous problem of separating overlapping patterns in noisy data parallels the challenge of detecting non-additive genetic effects masked by an additive model assumption. The adapted research idea is to treat genotype data as a signal and apply a suite of inheritance-mode-specific kernels (e.g., recessive, dominant, additive) as filters. By testing the association between the phenotype and these variously filtered genetic signals in a coordinated analysis—perhaps using a penalized regression or multi-kernel testing framework—the method can systematically identify which genetic model best fits each associated locus, relaxing the restrictive additive assumption.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Community Detection to Identify Novel T2D Gene Modules
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology and network science, where community detection algorithms are used to find emergent modules in species interaction networks beyond known food webs. The analogous solution involves applying algorithms like modularity optimization or Infomap to large-scale ecological interaction data. Adapted to T2D genetics, we treat the human molecular interactome as the ecosystem and T2D-associated genes as the focal species. By applying these unsupervised network community detection methods to the network neighborhood of T2D genes, we can identify novel, topologically dense gene modules. These modules represent candidate functional clusters involved in T2D pathogenesis that may not be captured by traditional, curated pathway databases, thereby revealing new biological networks for therapeutic targeting.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Seed and Soil' Paradigm to T2D: A Multi-Tissue Gene Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea applies the 'seed and soil' paradigm from cancer metastasis to challenge the predominant beta-cell-centric assumption in T2D pathogenesis. In oncology, solving metastasis required shifting focus from the primary tumor cell to the gene expression and microenvironment of distant organs that enabled colonization. Analogously, we propose to invert focus from beta cells to liver, muscle, and adipose tissue. The adapted research idea is to systematically profile the expression, epigenetic regulation, and inter-cellular signaling roles of identified T2D-risk genes specifically within these peripheral insulin-responsive tissues. The goal is to map how these genes collectively dysregulate local metabolic and inflammatory networks to create a systemic 'permissive soil' for diabetes, thereby identifying novel tissue-specific therapeutic targets beyond preserving beta-cell function.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Calibration of Linkage Disequilibrium Metrics Using Paleoclimatology Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'chronohorizon' method from paleoclimatology to solve the challenge of comparing Linkage Disequilibrium (LD) patterns across divergent non-human species. In paleoclimatology, disparate proxy records (e.g., from ice cores, tree rings, and ocean sediments) are aligned in time using widespread, well-dated marker events to build coherent global climate models. Similarly, for comparative genomics, we propose identifying 'genomic calibration points'—evolutionarily significant, dated events like speciations or whole-genome duplications that are fixed across a phylogeny. The tool's LD analysis framework would be applied to multiple model and non-model organisms, but the resulting LD decay curves and statistics would be temporally normalized using these calibration points. This adaptation enables a phylogenetically calibrated comparison, allowing researchers to distinguish whether patterns of LD are lineage-specific adaptations or reflect deeper evolutionary constraints shared across clades, thereby scaling the tool's insights from single-species analysis to evolutionary biology.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Deconvolution for Single-Cell Haplotype Phasing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the challenge of single-cell haplotype analysis. In seismology, blind deconvolution is used to recover the Earth's subsurface reflectivity series from a recorded seismic trace, which is a convolution of that series with an unknown source wavelet. Analogously, the observed single-cell genotype data can be viewed as a 'trace' resulting from the convolution of the true diploid haplotypes with an unknown, cell-specific technical profile encompassing amplification bias, allelic dropout, and sequencing errors. By adapting statistical blind deconvolution algorithms—which leverage assumptions about the sparsity and structure of haplotype blocks versus the properties of technical noise—we can develop methods to deconvolve and infer the underlying haplotype patterns directly from noisy, low-coverage single-cell genotype data, scaling analysis down to the fundamental cellular unit.

ideas.title: Modeling LD as a Continuous Topographic Map Using Geostatistical Kriging
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of discrete haplotype blocks by proposing to model linkage disequilibrium (LD) as a continuous landscape, analogous to how geostatistics models spatial resource distributions. In geostatistics, techniques like kriging use a variogram to model spatial autocorrelation as a continuous function of distance from sparse samples, creating a continuous topographic map of a resource. Adapted to genomics, SNP positions become coordinates on a one-dimensional genomic landscape. By constructing a variogram from population genotype data to model LD decay, we can apply kriging to interpolate a continuous LD surface across the genome. This approach predicts the correlation strength between any two genomic loci without imposing artificial block boundaries, fundamentally reframing LD from a segmented structure to a smooth, distance-dependent field.

ideas.title: A Multi-Resolution, Change-Point Informed Model for Continuous Linkage Disequilibrium Landscapes
ideas.description: This approach reframes LD modeling by combining the granularity of a continuous landscape with the practical need for interpretable structure. It first constructs a genome-wide continuous LD estimate using an adaptive kernel smoother whose parameters are informed by local genomic features. A statistical change-point analysis is then performed on this landscape to identify locations of significant discontinuity. The final model provides both the continuous LD surface for high-resolution analyses and a set of probabilistically defined segments, overcoming the computational and interpretive pitfalls of a purely continuous model while moving beyond the inaccuracies of rigid discrete blocks.

ideas.title: Apply Seismic Tomography Visualization to Haplotype Map Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the problem of visualizing complex genomic data. In seismology, seismic tomography solves the problem of visualizing hidden 3D subsurface structures by creating volumetric maps from wave propagation data. The analogous solution involves rendering these structures using isosurfaces and color gradients to represent physical properties. Adapted to genomics, we treat a haplotype map or a genomic region as a volumetric space. By applying similar 3D reconstruction and rendering techniques, we can transform population genetic metrics (e.g., Fst, π, LD) into a 3D 'genetic tomography' model. This allows researchers to visually interrogate the genomic landscape from any angle, identifying and isolating features like regions of recent positive selection or archaic introgression as distinct volumetric shapes, much like a geophysicist identifies a mantle plume.

ideas.title: Minimal Sensor Placement for Full Network Observability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the classic 'minimum PMU placement for full network observability' problem from electrical power systems to genomics. In power systems, engineers use graph theory and combinatorial optimization to find the smallest set of phasor measurement units (sensors) whose readings, propagated through the network's connectivity, can determine the complete state (voltage phasors) of all grid nodes. Analogously, we model major haplotypes as nodes in a similarity graph, where edges represent shared genomic segments or high linkage disequilibrium. Each SNP is a 'sensor' that provides a measurement (allele state) covering a subset of haplotypes it can differentiate. The objective is to algorithmically find the minimal set of SNPs such that, given known haplotype structures and patterns of linkage, the allelic configuration of every major haplotype in the set can be uniquely reconstructed. Solutions can draw from integer programming, greedy algorithms for minimum hitting set, or graph domination algorithms, tailored to the constraints of genetic inheritance and population haplotype diversity.

ideas.title: Declarative, Self-Validating Batch Framework for Reproducible Genomic Workflows
ideas.description: This system provides high-throughput, non-interactive analysis of genomic regions through a declarative specification layer that prevents common batch processing failures. Users define their analysis in a structured, validated configuration file that specifies all inputs, parameters, software containers, and computational resources. Before execution, the framework performs comprehensive validation of the entire workflow graph, checking data integrity, parameter sanity, and environment setup. It then executes the validated pipeline, automatically generating detailed provenance logs and standardized execution reports to guarantee reproducibility and robust error handling.

ideas.title: Applying Fluid Dynamics to Model Haplotype Flow and Turbulence
ideas.description: This idea challenges the static view of population haplotype patterns by drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics. In that field, the static description of a fluid is replaced by dynamic equations (e.g., Navier-Stokes) that model how properties like velocity and pressure evolve under forces. The analogous solution is to treat haplotypes not as fixed blocks but as a flowing 'genetic fluid.' The adapted research idea develops a theoretical framework where evolutionary forces (selection, demographic changes) act as pressures and viscosities within a set of continuum equations. This models how haplotype structure changes over time—predicting the 'turbulence' caused by strong selection or bottlenecks versus the 'laminar flow' of stable populations—thereby providing a dynamic, predictive model of haplotype evolution.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Resolution Map Tiling for Genome-Wide LD Network Visualization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-resolution tile pyramid solution from geographic information systems (GIS) to the challenge of visualizing genome-wide linkage disequilibrium (LD) networks. In GIS, rendering entire continents interactively is solved by pre-processing raw geographic data into a pyramid of image or vector tiles at different zoom levels, allowing a client to dynamically request only the data needed for the current view. We propose to apply this computational geometry and data management strategy to genomic LD. Efficient algorithms would be developed to pre-compute and store LD metrics (like r²) in a hierarchical, tiled data structure across multiple genomic resolutions. A visualization engine would then interactively query this structure, enabling researchers to seamlessly 'zoom' from a chromosome-wide overview of LD block structure down to fine-scale pairwise LD patterns, all within a scalable computational framework that avoids the bottleneck of loading genome-wide pairwise matrices into memory.

ideas.title: Network-Based Demographic Inference via Interactive LD Graph Simulation
ideas.description: This research direction develops an integrated software and modeling platform that fuses network visualization with demographic inference. It uses force-directed layouts to render observed LD patterns as interactive graphs, where nodes are SNPs and edges represent LD strength. Crucially, the platform connects this visualization to a simulation engine that allows users to propose demographic parameters (e.g., bottlenecks, admixture) and instantly visualize the predicted LD network structure their model generates. This creates a closed-loop, exploratory analysis tool where discrepancies between the simulated and observed LD graphs guide the iterative refinement of demographic hypotheses, making the inverse problem more intuitive and accessible.

ideas.title: Inferring Demographic History from LD Patterns Using Seismic Tomography Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem framework from seismic tomography to infer population history from linkage disequilibrium patterns. In seismology, Earth's internal structure is reconstructed by solving inverse problems using observed seismic wave travel times; here, we treat LD patterns as analogous 'genetic wave' measurements. The adapted solution involves: (1) developing forward models that simulate LD decay under parameterized demographic scenarios (population sizes, divergence times, migration rates), (2) implementing optimization algorithms that iteratively adjust demographic parameters to minimize the difference between simulated and observed genome-wide LD maps, and (3) creating a formal inversion framework where demographic history is the 'internal structure' to be reconstructed, complete with uncertainty quantification analogous to seismic tomography resolution analysis.

ideas.title: Batch Processing for Genomic Regions via Astronomical Data Pipeline Architecture
ideas.description: This idea proposes developing non-interactive, high-throughput analysis capabilities for many genomic regions by drawing an analogy to data processing pipelines in astronomy. In fields like astronomy, projects routinely process petabytes of image data from sky surveys using automated, modular pipeline architectures built on workflow systems (e.g., Apache Airflow). These systems chain discrete tasks into directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) for scalable, fault-tolerant, batch processing of millions of celestial objects. Adapted to genomics, the solution involves creating a suite of standalone, configurable command-line modules for core genomic region analysis tasks (region extraction, alignment, annotation, summarization). These modules can be linked into defined workflows via a workflow manager, allowing users to specify input regions and analysis parameters in a configuration file. The system then executes the entire pipeline in batch mode, providing automated, reproducible, and high-throughput processing with robust logging and error recovery, mirroring the efficiency of astronomical data pipelines.
